dimensions.summary: The project delivers far MORE than CLAUDE.md's stated "first deliverable" (which explicitly said "stop and show me after skeleton + crsf + failsafe + outputs") — all modules, a second firmware, a ground station, telemetry, gimbal, and tuning console are implemented and heavily native-tested, with a clear ROADMAP and bench runbook. That breadth is genuine and well-documented. But the "DONE" labels are all software-DONE, not hardware-verified: every real-world capability (CRSF in, PWM out, ADC, Hall, link2 UART, ELRS backchannel telemetry, WebRTC video) is bench-gated with no hardware run yet, and the ROADMAP/docs sometimes read as if features work end-to-end. The most concrete owner-surprise gaps: (1) the ground-station HUD documents and reads `armed`/`failsafe` telemetry that the car firmware never actually transmits, so a real link loss shows "Telemetry: sim" rather than a failsafe warning; (2) gear-count and drive-mode labels disagree across link2 doc (6 gears / "1=Gearbox"), firmware (4 gears), HUD (8 gears / "1=RACE"), and TELEMETRY.md ("1=Race"); (3) the entire telemetry return path is blocked on an unsolved exclusive-COM-port problem that is only "verify whether elrs-jc can forward." The demo/replay source masks gaps (2) and (3) by hand-filling every field. A beginner owner running `npm run demo` would reasonably believe the HUD shows live armed/failsafe/gear from the car; it does not yet, and may not without work the docs flag only as bench items.
well_designed: ROADMAP.md is unusually honest and specific: it records the original adversarial-review defects (A1 boot-full-lock, A2 arm gate) with file:line, marks D1.5 fixes as landed with regression-test notes, and keeps a triaged A3-A13 table — this is a real audit trail, not aspirational marketing.

The safety-critical order mandated by CLAUDE.md section 6 was actually followed: failsafe FSM with everReceivedFrame_ latch, ArmGate requiring fresh-neutral after every episode, ESC boot-arm hold, and battery as monitoring-only are all implemented and referenced in src/main.cpp (lines 311-334) with the reasoning inline.

docs/D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md is a genuinely strong 11-phase gated runbook with the golden rule (wheels off / no ESC power until failsafe+arm proven live, Phase 5) enforced as an explicit gate before Phase 7, and every accumulated bench-verify item from the design reviews consolidated in order.

The viewer-only ground-station safety decision is consistent everywhere (README.md lines 15-20, ROADMAP item 4): the app never commands the car, elrs-joystick-control keeps the control link, and a zero-code VLC + elrs-jc fallback is documented as always-available — a sound gift-day risk posture.

link2 protocol is genuinely documented (docs/link2_protocol.md) with a worked hex example pinned by a golden-frame test, and CLAUDE.md section 2.7's framed-message requirement (start byte + payload + checksum) is met.

The telemetry-transport reasoning in docs/TELEMETRY.md is thorough and self-aware: it explains why standard CRSF frames over ELRS beat MSP and WiFi/ESP-NOW (WROOM is 2.4GHz-only, video AP is 5.8GHz), and pins every emitted frame with an identical golden vector in both firmware and ground-station tests.

findings.title: Ground-station HUD documents and reads `armed`/`failsafe` telemetry the car firmware never transmits
findings.evidence: hud.js:206 `if (telem.failsafe) { linkEl.textContent = 'LINK LOST'; ... }`; docs/TELEMETRY.md lines 11-12 list `armed` and `failsafe` as contract fields; hud.js:6 comment claims CAR-SIDE TRUTH includes `armed, failsafe`. But the firmware only emits three CRSF frame types in main.cpp:264-294: BATTERY 0x08, GPS 0x02 (groundspeed only), and FLIGHTMODE 0x21 with string "G%u M%u E%u" (main.cpp:287) — no armed, no failsafe bit anywhere. shared/crsfTelemetry.js populates only linkQualityPct/battery/speed/gear/driveMode/ersPct; grep shows armed/failsafe set ONLY in shared/replaySource.js:52-53 (the demo source).
findings.why_matters: The owner's primary safety-status indicator on the HUD (LINK LOST / armed state) will never light from the real car. Worse, a genuine link loss stops all serial frames, so telemetry goes stale and the HUD falls back to `linkEl.textContent = 'Telemetry: sim'` (hud.js:209) showing simulated live-looking values — the opposite of a failsafe warning. `npm run demo` hides this because replaySource hand-fills armed/failsafe, so it looks fully wired.
findings.expected: HUD shows the car's real armed/failsafe state, or docs make clear these are not delivered over the CRSF backchannel and only LQ approximates link loss.
findings.suspected: armed/failsafe are documented+read but never encoded by the firmware telemetry path; they work only in the replay/demo source. On real hardware they are permanently undefined.
findings.how_to_verify: Grep the firmware for any CRSF frame carrying armed/failsafe (none exists); trace shared/crsfTelemetry.js — it has no branch that sets telem.armed or telem.failsafe. Confirm hud.js:205-210 fallback behavior on a stalled source.
findings.fix_direction: Either encode armed/failsafe into the FLIGHTMODE status string (e.g. add flags to "G3 M2 E55") and parse them in shared/crsf.js, or remove armed/failsafe from the TELEMETRY.md contract and hud.js comment and drive LINK LOST from a linkQuality==0-plus-staleness heuristic. At minimum document that real link loss reverts the HUD to sim, not to a warning.

findings.title: Gear count disagrees across link2 doc (6), firmware (4), and HUD (8) — live telemetry gear will render on the wrong ring
findings.evidence: lib/gearbox/include/gearbox/Gearbox.hpp:26 `uint8_t numGears = 4;`; docs/link2_protocol.md:39 `gear | 1-based display gear, 1…6.`; ground-station renderer/hud.js:35 `let FEEL = { gears: 8, ... }`. The firmware sends the real gear (1..4) in the FLIGHTMODE string (main.cpp:287, displayGear = currentGear()+1), and hud.js:176 prefers telemetry driveMode/gear when live.
findings.why_matters: When live telemetry supplies gear ≤4, the HUD's gear ring, caps table (hud.js:39 computeCaps over 8 gears) and redline logic are built for 8 gears, so the car's top gear (4) displays as a mid-ring gear and the speed-cap-per-gear math is meaningless. The owner sees a gear indicator that doesn't match the car. link2 board #2 (soundlight) also reads gear expecting 1..6 per the copied doc.
findings.expected: One gear count shared across firmware, link2 doc, and HUD.
findings.suspected: Three independent gear counts; the ROADMAP itself (item 8, line 254) admits 'the display model even used a different gear count' as the reason for sending real gear, but never reconciled FEEL.gears=8 with numGears=4.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare the three cited lines. Run npm run demo and observe the gear ring vs a live G4 value.
findings.fix_direction: Set FEEL.gears and link2 doc's gear range to match gearbox numGears (4), or make the HUD gear ring size adapt to the received gear range. Ideally push the gear count through the shared feel-constants the ground station already imports.

findings.title: Entire telemetry return path is blocked on an unsolved exclusive-COM-port problem, documented as 'verify' not 'works'
findings.evidence: docs/TELEMETRY.md:52-62 'the FT232 COM port is exclusive ... elrs-joystick-control holds it ... our app can't open it a second time' with three unresolved options (option 1 'Verify whether it can', option 2 com0com, option 3 not recommended). D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md:146-150 same gate. ROADMAP.md:220-221 'Bench-gated: the FT232 COM port is held exclusively ... read telemetry via its forward flag (verify) or a com0com splitter.'
findings.why_matters: ROADMAP items 5 and 8 are marked '✅ DONE 2026-07-03' but the delivered capability (any car telemetry reaching the HUD) hinges on a Windows COM-sharing question that has not been answered — it may require elrs-joystick-control to have a forwarding flag it might not have, or a com0com install the owner must set up. If neither works, the HUD gets zero live telemetry and only the demo source ever shows overlays. 'DONE' overstates a path that is code-complete but end-to-end unproven and possibly infeasible on the intended single-port setup.
findings.expected: A verified way to get CRSF telemetry off the FT232 while elrs-joystick-control owns it, or DONE downgraded to 'implemented, transport unproven'.
findings.suspected: The transport is unresolved; the firmware emits frames but no verified reader exists on the shared port. serialport is also a native module needing electron-rebuild (SETUP.md:47), an extra fragility.
findings.how_to_verify: Check whether elrs-joystick-control actually exposes a telemetry forward/UDP/stdout flag (external tool, not in these repos). Confirm com0com works with the FT232 driver. Neither verifiable from the repos alone.
findings.fix_direction: Confirm elrs-joystick-control's forwarding capability before relying on it; if absent, validate the com0com splitter path on the actual Windows machine and document exact steps; treat as HIGH bench risk alongside the H.265 codec risk.

findings.title: Pin-map reconciliation against the wiring atlas is a documented obligation with no evidence it was completed
findings.evidence: CLAUDE.md section 1: 'Pin map — STARTING PROPOSAL. Reconcile against my wiring atlas (docs/w17_wiring_assembly_atlas.html) before locking it.' PinMap.hpp header comment says pins are 'transcribed from CLAUDE.md section 1' — i.e. from the proposal, not reconciled against the atlas. The atlas HTML exists in docs/. No doc states the reconciliation was done; D8 Phase 4 defers channel-map/pin confirmation to the bench.
findings.why_matters: If the atlas (the authoritative wiring source) disagrees with the proposed pins — e.g. gimbal on GPIO19/23, link2 on 25/26 — the firmware will drive the wrong pins and bring-up burns a bench session. The obligation was flagged by the owner as a pre-lock step and there is no evidence it happened; it was pushed to bench verification instead.
findings.expected: PinMap.hpp reconciled against docs/w17_wiring_assembly_atlas.html before flashing, per CLAUDE.md section 1.
findings.suspected: Pins are still the untouched CLAUDE.md proposal; reconciliation deferred to Phase 4 bench, contrary to the 'before you lock it' instruction.
findings.how_to_verify: Open docs/w17_wiring_assembly_atlas.html and cross-check every signal against PinMap.hpp; confirm gimbal 19/23, link2 25/26, and the strapping-pin avoidance still hold.
findings.fix_direction: Read the atlas HTML and diff its pin assignments against PinMap.hpp; lock any discrepancies before D8. (Out of scope to modify here — flag for the owner.)

findings.title: Drive-mode label 1 is inconsistent: firmware/link2 say 'Gearbox', HUD and TELEMETRY.md say 'RACE'/'Race'
findings.evidence: docs/link2_protocol.md:43 `driveMode | 0 = Training, 1 = Gearbox, 2 = Gearbox+ERS`; src/main.cpp:63-64 comment `0 = Training, 1 = Gearbox ... 2 = Gearbox+ERS`; ground-station renderer/hud.js:24-26 DRIVE_MODES has `{ label: 'RACE' }` at index 1; docs/TELEMETRY.md:16 `driveMode | 0=Training 1=Race 2=ERS`. Numbers match everywhere; only the name for mode 1 differs.
findings.why_matters: Cosmetic but confusing: the handset/serial narration says 'Gearbox' while the HUD badge says 'RACE'. An owner debugging modes sees two names for the same detent. Numbers are consistent so no functional break.
findings.expected: One label per drive mode across all surfaces.
findings.suspected: Two naming conventions coexist; low risk because the wire value (0/1/2) is authoritative and consistent.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare the four cited files.
findings.fix_direction: Pick one label for mode 1 (Gearbox or Race) and use it in hud.js DRIVE_MODES and TELEMETRY.md, or firmware comment/doc.

findings.title: D8 bench runbook lists the camera gimbal as 'decoded, unwired' but it is fully wired in main.cpp
findings.evidence: docs/D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md:163 'Deferred / optional (won't block the gift): Gimbal pan/tilt (decoded, unwired)'. But src/main.cpp:91-92 create panPwm/tiltPwm, :97-98 panServo/tiltServo, :183-184 begin() them, :189-190 center them, and :372-373 drive them every control tick from controls.pan/controls.tilt. ROADMAP.md item 7 (line 240) correctly marks gimbal '✅ DONE 2026-07-03' and D8 Phase 7b (lines 108-116) also covers the wired gimbal.
findings.why_matters: Internal doc contradiction: the same runbook's Phase 7b treats the gimbal as wired and testable, while its Deferred section calls it unwired. An owner reading the Deferred list might skip gimbal bring-up or think GPIO19/23 are free.
findings.expected: D8 Deferred list consistent with the wired implementation.
findings.suspected: Stale line 163 left over from before item 7 landed.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md:163 against main.cpp:372-373 and Phase 7b.
findings.fix_direction: Remove 'Gimbal pan/tilt (decoded, unwired)' from D8's Deferred list; it is implemented and has its own Phase 7b.

findings.title: GPS-frame groundspeed telemetry: km/h*10 integer conversion truncates and caps low-speed resolution; unverified against real ELRS relay
findings.evidence: main.cpp:275 `const uint16_t speedKmhX10 = static_cast<uint16_t>((mmPerSec * 36u) / 1000u);` — a 1/10-scale car moving ~2 m/s (2000 mm/s) yields (2000*36)/1000 = 72 => 7.2 km/h; but at 0.28 m/s (280 mm/s) => (280*36)/1000 = 10 => 1.0 km/h, and below ~28 mm/s the result truncates to 0. TELEMETRY.md:66 documents 0.1 km/h units. Whether real ELRS/handset relays a GPS 0x02 frame with only groundspeed populated (lat/lon/sats=0) is bench-unverified (D8 Phase 10).
findings.why_matters: Low-speed ground speed shown on the HUD will be coarse/zero, and it is unproven that ELRS relays a synthetic GPS frame with zeroed lat/lon without the ground TX rejecting or mangling it. Minor for a HUD gauge; flagged because the whole GPS-as-speed choice is clever but hardware-contingent.
findings.expected: Speed readable at the low speeds a 1/10 car actually reaches; ELRS confirmed to relay the frame.
findings.suspected: Resolution is fine at speed but truncates near zero; relay behavior of a partially-populated GPS frame is untested.
findings.how_to_verify: Bench: emit the GPS frame, confirm the ground TX forwards it and the HUD shows plausible km/h; check whether some handsets require valid fix/sats.
findings.fix_direction: Confirm ELRS relays GPS 0x02 with zeroed fix; if speed resolution matters at low end, keep mm/s in a different field or scale differently. Bench item, not a code fix now.

findings.title: CLAUDE.md's stated first-deliverable gate ('stop and show me' after skeleton/crsf/failsafe/outputs) was not honored — everything was built in one pass
findings.evidence: CLAUDE.md section 8: 'First deliverable — stop and show me after this ... Then pause and show me so I can verify the foundation before you build gearbox / channel map / telemetry / link2 / Wokwi.' ROADMAP.md shows D2-D8 plus Phase-2 items 1-8 all completed 2026-07-02/03, i.e. the entire system was built past the requested pause point.
findings.why_matters: The owner explicitly asked to review the foundation before more was built ('I review every diff', section 5). Building all of it before the pause means the foundational review the owner wanted did happen (ROADMAP records the A1-A13 adversarial review) but the incremental-diff-review workflow the owner requested was compressed. Low severity because the review was thorough and documented, but it is a deviation from an explicit process instruction.
findings.expected: Pause after deliverable #1 per section 8.
findings.suspected: The pause was superseded by an approved continuous build (ROADMAP 'order agreed 2026-07-02'); the owner appears to have consented after the fact, but CLAUDE.md still reads as the standing instruction.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare CLAUDE.md section 8 against the ROADMAP completion dates.
findings.fix_direction: None needed if the owner consented; note that CLAUDE.md section 8's gate is now stale and could mislead a future contributor.

open_questions: Does elrs-joystick-control actually expose a telemetry-forwarding flag (UDP/log/stdout)? The whole HUD telemetry path depends on it or on com0com; neither is verifiable from these repos. (docs/TELEMETRY.md:57-61)

Does docs/w17_wiring_assembly_atlas.html agree with PinMap.hpp on every signal? The atlas is the authoritative source per CLAUDE.md section 1 but I did not diff it exhaustively; a mismatch on any actuator pin is a bring-up hazard.

Will a real ELRS/handset relay a CRSF GPS 0x02 frame that has only groundspeed set (lat/lon/sats=0), and a custom FLIGHTMODE 0x21 status string 'G3 M2 E55', without rejecting or reformatting them? The whole 'send everything over standard relayed frames' claim (TELEMETRY.md) is bench-unverified.
Should the HUD's armed/failsafe indicators be driven from something real, or is the owner content that link loss silently reverts the HUD to simulated values?

hw_validation_items.item: Verify whether elrs-joystick-control can forward CRSF telemetry off the FT232, or install/validate com0com — without this the HUD gets zero live telemetry (armed/failsafe/speed/gear/battery/LQ all stay simulated).

Diff docs/w17_wiring_assembly_atlas.html against lib/config PinMap.hpp for every signal (steering 13, ESC 14, DRS 18, gimbal 19/23, battery 34, Hall 35, CRSF 16/17, link2 25/26) before flashing to hardware.

Confirm the ground TX relays a partially-populated GPS 0x02 (groundspeed only) and a custom FLIGHTMODE 0x21 status string end-to-end to the HUD; this validates ROADMAP items 5/8 which are marked DONE but hardware-unproven.
Confirm the camera emits H.264 (not H.265) or that the H.265->H.264 transcode path works — SETUP.md's #1 risk, gates all WebRTC video in the HUD.
On a real link loss, observe what the HUD shows (expected: reverts to 'Telemetry: sim' with simulated values, NOT a failsafe warning) and decide if that is acceptable gift-day behavior.

dimensions.dimension: Repository-level architecture across the 3 repos (control-fw / soundlight-fw / ground-station): responsibility split, duplication, and drift risk
dimensions.summary: The three-repo split is coherent in intent — control-fw owns the CRSF-in / actuator-out / link2-out sender, soundlight-fw is a link2 receiver, ground-station is a read-only telemetry HUD — and the HAL-seam architecture is genuinely clean: I grepped every pure-logic library in both firmwares and found ZERO Arduino/ESP/driver/FreeRTOS includes (the only "esp32/esp_" hits are inside comments). Pure logic includes only <cstdint>-class headers and other HAL interfaces, so the native Unity test builds are legitimately hardware-free, reinforced by explicit test_build_src=no + lib_ignore of the *_hal_esp32 libs. The serious problem is DRIFT: three copies of the same wire truth (link2 codec, CRSF byte layout, ERS/gear numbers) are maintained by hand across repos with NO CI check, NO submodule, NO shared package keeping them in sync — the repos are fully independent git remotes. The link2 codec is byte-identical today only by luck of copy-paste; the CRSF C++ and CRSF JS are parallel reimplementations that are NOT tested against literally-shared golden vectors (each test hand-rebuilds its own frames), and a code comment overstates the coupling by claiming "the firmware's golden vectors are reused as tests." The soundlight link2 library.json is a verbatim copy whose description is now factually wrong for that repo. Confirmed contradictions in drive-mode labels and gear counts persist across the doc/code surface. None of this is hardware-gated; all is verifiable statically.
well_designed: HAL-seam is genuinely leak-free: grep across lib/crsf, channels, gearbox, failsafe, outputs, telemetry, link2, ers, hal, config, settings, console (control-fw) and enginesim, lights, soundsynth, link2, link2monitor (soundlight-fw) for Arduino.h/<driver/>/freertos/ledc/HardwareSerial/WiFi.h returns only comment-text matches, no real includes. Pure logic is authentically host-compilable.

platformio.ini native env is defensively configured against HAL leaks: control-fw [env:native] sets test_build_src = no (so src/main.cpp with Arduino.h never compiles into tests) and lib_ignore lists all five *_hal_esp32 libs as belt-and-suspenders; soundlight-fw mirrors this. Comments explain the 'why' (lib/... : platformio.ini).

link2 is cleanly split into a pure codec (Link2Codec.hpp/.cpp, Link2Frame.hpp — frameworks:'*', platforms:'*') and a HAL-touching Link2Sender (control-fw only, gated behind lib/hal). The codec is genuinely liftable, which is why the copy into soundlight-fw was mechanically possible and stayed byte-identical.

feelConstants.js drift guard is numerically CORRECT: shared/feelConstants.js says ERS_DEPLOY_PCT_PER_SEC=26 / HARVEST=11 / BOOST=1.18, and lib/ers/include/ers/ErsSystem.hpp actually declares deployRatePermille=260, harvestBrakeRatePermille=110, boostBonusPermille=180 — consistent by the documented permille->pct/sec relation, and test/replay.test.js hard-asserts the JS numbers so a change on the JS side fails CI.

Clear one-way responsibility flow with documented seams: crsf library.json declares 'No hardware dependency'; link2 library.json declares it depends only on hal via Link2Sender; console/ConsoleRunner composes Console + hal/ICharIO + hal/ISettingsStore behind interfaces so main.cpp holds the only concrete impls. The dependency direction (logic -> interface, impl -> interface) is respected throughout.

The CRSF telemetry byte layouts are at least conceptually cross-checked: control-fw test_build_battery_frame_bytes uses 7.9V=0x004F/72% and ground-station crsfTelemetry.test.js batteryFrame() reproduces the identical 7.9V=0x004F values with a comment pointing at the firmware test, so a divergence in the battery layout would likely be caught by a human reading both — even though it is not machine-enforced.

findings.title: No mechanism prevents lib/link2 from silently diverging between control-fw and soundlight-fw
findings.evidence: diff of Link2Frame.hpp, Link2Codec.hpp, Link2Codec.cpp, library.json between the two repos = IDENTICAL today, but three separate git remotes (github.com/.../w17-control-fw, .../w17-soundlight-fw), no .gitmodules in either, no shared package, and neither .github/workflows/ci.yml references the other repo. The codec was copy-pasted (soundlight lib/link2 lacks Link2Sender.hpp/.cpp which control-fw has).
findings.why_matters: link2 is the ONLY runtime contract between the two ESP32 boards. If the sender (control-fw) changes the payload layout, CRC domain, start byte, or a flag-bit meaning and the receiver copy is not updated in lockstep, the two boards silently disagree: brake lights / engine sound / ERS whine key off wrong bytes with no build error and no test failure, because each repo tests only its own copy. This is the single highest drift risk in the system.
findings.expected: A single source of truth (git submodule, shared package, or a CI job that hashes/diffs the two link2 trees and fails on mismatch) so the sender and receiver cannot diverge undetected.
findings.suspected: Each repo owns an editable copy; the only thing keeping them equal is developer discipline. A one-line edit in either repo passes that repo's CI (147/147 or 40/40) while breaking the inter-board protocol.
findings.how_to_verify: Change one byte-offset constant or a flag bit in control-fw/lib/link2 only, run both repos' native tests: both still pass. On hardware the boards would then misinterpret frames.
findings.fix_direction: Add a CI step (in either or both repos) that fetches the counterpart link2 tree and fails if the shared files differ, OR vendor link2 as a git submodule / shared PlatformIO library so there is one copy. Byte-level round-trip vectors shared between both would also catch it.

findings.title: CRSF decoder is reimplemented (C++ in control-fw, JS in ground-station) with NO shared golden vectors; a comment overstates the coupling
findings.evidence: shared/crsf.js header comment: 'ported FAITHFULLY from the firmware ... The firmware's golden vectors are reused as tests.' But test/crsf.test.js and test/crsfTelemetry.test.js define their OWN JS buildFrame() and hand-write payloads; nothing imports or reads any firmware .hpp/.cpp or a shared vector file (grep for 'control-fw'/'.hpp'/'golden'/'CrsfParser' in test/ and shared/ finds only comment strings). The C++ side (lib/crsf/test) independently hand-builds frames via CrsfFrameBuilder. Two parallel implementations, two parallel hand-maintained test sets.
findings.why_matters: The whole ELRS backchannel telemetry path depends on the JS decoder byte-for-byte matching what the firmware emits (battery 0x08, GPS 0x02, FLIGHTMODE 0x21, link stats 0x14). If either side's byte layout, endianness, or CRC domain drifts, the HUD silently shows wrong speed/battery/gear. Because the vectors are duplicated by hand rather than literally shared, the tests give false confidence — they can both pass while the two implementations disagree on a case neither test happens to cover. The comment claiming vectors are 'reused' is misleading to any future maintainer who trusts it.
findings.expected: A single machine-shared set of golden frame vectors (e.g. a committed hex/JSON fixture the firmware test emits and the JS test consumes, or vice versa) so a layout change on one side fails the other side's test.
findings.suspected: Agreement is currently coincidental and human-verified. Note also a benign-but-undocumented semantic divergence: JS decodeFrame() accepts ANY frame type and any length>=2 (needed for the multi-type backchannel), while C++ crsf::decodeFrame() rejects non-RC_CHANNELS types with UnsupportedType and requires the exact RC length — same function name, same DecodeResult enum, deliberately different contract.
findings.how_to_verify: Introduce a deliberate off-by-one in the GPS speed byte offset in shared/crsf.js decodeGps: all 21 vitest still pass unless a test happens to hit it; no firmware test can catch it.
findings.fix_direction: Emit golden frames from the firmware builder into a committed fixture and drive both test suites from it, or add a small cross-repo vector file. Separately, either rename the JS decodeFrame/DecodeResult to signal it is the multi-type backchannel variant, or document the intentional contract difference in shared/crsf.js.

findings.title: Drive-mode labels and gear counts are inconsistent across repos with no shared enum
findings.evidence: driveMode 1/2 labelled 'Gearbox'/'Gearbox+ERS' in control-fw/lib/link2/include/link2/Link2Frame.hpp (lines 31,65) and docs/link2_protocol.md line 43, but 'RACE'/'ERS' in ground-station renderer/hud.js DRIVE_MODES (lines 24-28) and docs/TELEMETRY.md line 16. Gear count: docs/link2_protocol.md line 39 says gear '1…6'; control-fw lib/gearbox/include/gearbox/Gearbox.hpp line 26 numGears=4; ground-station hud.js line 35 FEEL.gears=8. main.cpp line 287 emits the car gear into the FLIGHTMODE 'G%u' string with no shared bound.
findings.why_matters: Numbers match (0/1/2) so the HUD won't crash, but the human-facing labels contradict across the authoritative doc and the HUD, and the gear ceiling is triple-defined (4 real gears, doc says up to 6, HUD dial shows 8). A reviewer or the gift recipient sees a mode/gear count on-screen that the car does not implement. It is a coherence/QA defect and a symptom of no shared contract module.
findings.expected: One canonical drive-mode label set and one gear-count constant referenced (or explicitly mirrored with a drift guard) by firmware doc, firmware code, and HUD.
findings.suspected: Independent authoring per repo. The HUD's 8-gear dial and RACE/ERS naming were themed for display and never reconciled with the firmware's 4-gear Gearbox/Gearbox+ERS reality; docs/link2_protocol.md's '1..6' matches neither.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare the four cited locations directly; note the gearbox default of 4 vs doc 6 vs HUD 8.
findings.fix_direction: Pick canonical labels + gear count, update Link2Frame.hpp/link2_protocol.md/hud.js/TELEMETRY.md together, and add a feelConstants-style guard test for the gear count on the HUD side.

findings.title: control-fw leaves platform espressif32 UNPINNED while soundlight-fw pins ~7.0.1 — cross-firmware toolchain skew
findings.evidence: control-fw platformio.ini [env:esp32dev] 'platform = espressif32' (no version); soundlight-fw [env:esp32dev] 'platform = espressif32 @ ~7.0.1' with a comment explaining the pin is needed for legacy I2S driver semantics.
findings.why_matters: The two boards share the link2 codec source and are meant to behave as one system, but they can build against different Arduino-ESP32 cores. An unpinned control-fw will silently follow whatever espressif32 is latest at build time, which can change LEDC/ADC/UART behavior between a bench build and a later rebuild near the 2026-07-21 deadline — exactly when you least want a surprise. Reproducibility of the delivered gift firmware is not guaranteed.
findings.expected: Both firmwares pin the platform version (ideally the same major), so builds are reproducible and the two boards use compatible cores.
findings.suspected: Oversight — soundlight-fw got a pin because I2S forced the issue; control-fw was never pinned because nothing forced it, leaving it exposed.
findings.how_to_verify: Read both platformio.ini files; note the version specifier present in one and absent in the other.
findings.fix_direction: Pin control-fw to a specific espressif32 version (match soundlight if the shared behavior matters) and record it.

findings.title: soundlight-fw lib/link2/library.json description is a stale verbatim copy that is factually wrong for that repo
findings.evidence: soundlight lib/link2/library.json description: 'Framed vehicle-state message to the sound/light ESP32 #2 ... only Link2Sender touches hal.' — but soundlight-fw has NO Link2Sender file (find -name '*Link2Sender*' returns nothing there; it only ships Link2Codec + is consumed by lib/link2monitor as a receiver). It also declares dependencies:{hal:*} despite the sender (the only hal-touching part) not being present.
findings.why_matters: The description describes the SENDER role in a repo that is purely a RECEIVER. It is direct evidence the file was copy-pasted rather than authored, and it will mislead anyone reading the board-#2 library metadata about which side owns the frame. The spurious hal dependency also slightly muddies the receiver's dependency graph.
findings.expected: A description reflecting board #2's receive/decode role, and dependencies matching the files actually shipped in that repo's copy.
findings.suspected: Verbatim copy artifact; harmless at runtime but confirms the no-sync copy-paste workflow that finding #1 warns about.
findings.how_to_verify: Read both library.json files: byte-identical, yet one repo lacks the Link2Sender the text references.
findings.fix_direction: Edit the soundlight copy's description/deps to the receiver role — but ideally fold into the finding #1 single-source-of-truth fix so the two library.json files legitimately differ by role instead of being wrongly identical.

findings.title: feelConstants.js and crsf.js reference a firmware source path/claim that is stale, weakening the drift guards' traceability
findings.evidence: feelConstants.js and replay.test.js both cite 'w17-control-fw/lib/ers/ErsSystem.hpp' as the source of truth; that file does exist, but the ERS constants also live in the ErsConfig struct and the guard test hard-codes 26/11/1.18 with no automated link to the firmware value 260/110/180. The crsf.js 'golden vectors are reused' claim (see finding #2) is outright false. These comments are the only thing pointing a maintainer at the real source.
findings.why_matters: The drift guards work today only because a human keeps the hand-copied numbers in step with a path named in a comment. If the firmware ErsConfig defaults change, the JS guard test keeps passing (it asserts the OLD agreed numbers), so 'guard' actually just freezes a snapshot; it cannot detect firmware-side drift. Combined with the misleading crsf.js comment, the cross-repo comments overstate how much is actually enforced.
findings.expected: Guard tests that fail when the firmware value changes (shared fixture), and comments that accurately describe what is and is not enforced.
findings.suspected: The guards are snapshot assertions, not true cross-repo checks; the accompanying comments imply stronger coupling than exists.
findings.how_to_verify: Change deployRatePermille in ErsSystem.hpp from 260 to 300: control-fw tests may still pass and ground-station replay.test.js definitely still passes, so the HUD ERS drain rate silently no longer matches the car.
findings.fix_direction: Either accept snapshot semantics and correct the comments to say so, or drive the JS constants from a firmware-emitted fixture; fix the crsf.js comment regardless.

open_questions: Is the copy-paste of lib/link2 into soundlight-fw intended to be permanent (two independently-evolving copies) or a temporary bootstrap toward a shared submodule? The library.json description ('liftable into the board-#2 project wholesale') suggests the former, which makes finding #1 more urgent.

Which gear count is authoritative for the delivered car — the gearbox default of 4, the protocol doc's 1..6, or the HUD's 8? This determines what the FLIGHTMODE 'G%u' string and the HUD dial should show.
Which drive-mode label set ships to the recipient: Gearbox/Gearbox+ERS (firmware/doc) or RACE/ERS (HUD)? The ground-station is the user-facing surface, so its labels are what the recipient reads.

Should the ground-station's CRSF backchannel decodeFrame keep the CRSF sync-byte name/DecodeResult shared with the firmware RC parser despite an intentionally different contract, or be renamed to avoid the trap noted in finding #2?

hw_validation_items.item: On the bench (both ESP32s powered, common ground, GPIO25->GPIO16), confirm the soundlight receiver decodes a live link2 frame from control-fw identically to the sender's intent (throttle%, brake bit, ERS-deploying bit, gear, driveMode) — this is the real check that the two hand-maintained link2 copies have not drifted, since no CI enforces it.

With the ELRS backchannel live (FT232 released to the ground station), confirm the HUD's shared/crsf.js decodes real firmware-emitted BATTERY/GPS/FLIGHTMODE frames with correct values (battery volts, wheel speed km/h, gear/mode/ERS from 'G# M# E#') — validates the parallel-reimplementation agreement that tests cannot fully guarantee.
Confirm the gear number the car actually emits in the FLIGHTMODE string never exceeds what the HUD dial (FEEL.gears) and the protocol doc claim, once the canonical gear count is decided.

dimensions.summary: The pin maps in both firmwares are internally consistent, match CLAUDE.md sections 1/7 exactly, and are electrically sound: no strapping pins (0/2/12/15/5) are used as driven outputs, input-only GPIO34/35 are used only for analog sense and an externally-pulled Hall input, and the LEDC 5-servo allocation (channels 0-4, all 50 Hz/16-bit) has no timer conflict because every servo shares the identical timer config. The battery-divider math (37/10 ratio for 27k/10k, 8.4 V -> ~2.27 V pin, within the 11 dB ~2.45 V ceiling) is correct, the ESC signal-only + isolated-BEC rule is documented and enforced procedurally, and the bench-bringup runbook (D8) is unusually thorough about pre-power fixes. The wiring atlas is NOT a pin-number authority (it explicitly says "pin numbers are illustrative — your firmware defines the real ones"), so the "three-way reconcile" reduces to firmware-vs-CLAUDE.md-vs-BOM, which agree. Real risks are the classic ones that only surface with hardware: WS2812 3.3 V->5 V data logic-high relies on a documented-but-marginal 1N5819 diode-drop rather than a proper level shifter; the ESC/servo signal pins float high-Z for ~tens of ms at boot before setup() drives them; and Hall EMI double-counting near the brushless motor is anticipated but unproven. None of these block bring-up; all are called out in the docs. No hardware-damaging or unsafe-motion defect was found in this dimension.
well_designed: PinMap.hpp (control-fw) matches CLAUDE.md section 1 pin-for-pin and keeps every GPIO in one header with per-pin provenance comments; ESP32 #2 PinMap.hpp is fully independent and explicitly notes 'All non-strapping, none input-only.' No strapping pin (GPIO0/2/12/15/5) is used as a driven output on either board (control outputs = 13/14/18/19/23; soundlight = I2S 25/26/22, LED 4).

LEDC allocation in src/main.cpp lines 88-92 gives the 5 servos distinct channels 0-4 with identical 50 Hz / 16-bit config (Esp32LedcPwm.hpp kFrequencyHz=50, kResolutionBits=16), so even though Arduino-ESP32 pairs channels onto shared timers (timer=channel/2), the shared timers carry identical frequency/resolution and cannot conflict.

Esp32LedcPwm::begin(initialPulseMicros) (Esp32LedcPwm.hpp:23, .cpp:9-13) immediately commands a known-safe pulse on attach, and setup() (main.cpp:180-184) passes center/neutral/closed for each channel, closing the 'LEDC idles at duty 0 = no pulses until first write' gap the header comment documents (finding A4).

Battery-divider math is correct and overflow-guarded: BatteryConfig dividerNum=37/dividerDen=10 = (27k+10k)/10k = 3.7 (BatteryMonitor.hpp:11-12), 8.4 V -> 2.27 V pin sits safely under the 11 dB ~2.45 V usable ceiling, and valid() (lines 40-41) proves the u32 conversion and u16 result cannot overflow. Uses analogReadMilliVolts (eFuse-calibrated) with a 4-read burst to fight the ~7.3k divider source impedance (Esp32BatteryAdc.cpp:13-19).

Esp32HallPulseCounter marks the ISR and trampoline IRAM_ATTR (Esp32HallPulseCounter.cpp:15-21) so it survives flash-cache-disabled windows, uses lock-free std::atomic<uint32_t> for count/period, a 2 ms software lockout for EMI/bounce debounce, and correctly configures GPIO35 as plain INPUT with no internal pull (relying on the external 10k as CLAUDE.md 7 specifies).

CRSF UART is opened NOT inverted at 420000 8N1 on Serial2/GPIO16-17 (Esp32CrsfUart.cpp:12, kCrsfBaud=420000), matching standard ELRS CRSF and CLAUDE.md; link2 uses Serial1 with rxPin=-1 remapped TX to GPIO25 (Esp32Link2Uart.cpp:10), correctly avoiding UART1's default flash-adjacent pins. All three UARTs (0 console, 1 link2, 2 CRSF) are non-conflicting.

docs/D8_BENCH_BRINGUP.md Phase 0 enumerates every pre-power electrical fix (ESC red-wire isolation, 27k/10k divider, 100 nF on GPIO34, Hall 5V/3.3V pull-up, WS2812 330R+1000uF+1N5819, servo-rail 1000uF, common ground) and gates ESC power behind proven failsafe (Phase 5), which is exactly the right hardware-bring-up discipline.

soundlight PinMap.hpp documents the MAX98357A straps (GAIN floating=9dB, SD_MODE high=(L+R)/2) as non-driven and notes duplicated-stereo transmission so any SD_MODE selection sounds identical; I2S config (Esp32I2sAudio.cpp) uses standard master-TX 16-bit with tx_desc_auto_clear to avoid buffer-underrun screech.

WS2812 power budget is validated at compile time: LightConfig::valid() (LightRenderer.hpp:59-65) proves 30 px * ~17 mA (2ch amber at maxBrightness 110/255) ~= 517 mA stays under the 900 mA budget, well within a 5 A rail.

findings.title: WS2812 3.3 V data into 5 V strip relies on a marginal diode-drop, not a real level shifter
findings.evidence: PinMap.hpp:26-28 comment: 'WS2812B strip data, through the 330R series resistor at the strip end (build sheet fixes: 1000uF across strip 5V/GND + 1N5819 on strip VDD).' 00_BUILD_SHEET.md bench fix #4: '1N5819 series diode on strip VDD (-> ~3.0V threshold) *or* 74AHCT125 shifter.' D8 Phase 0 lists '1N5819 on strip VDD (or a level shifter)'.
findings.why_matters: WS2812B logic-high threshold is ~0.7*Vdd. Driven from a full 5 V strip, 0.7*5=3.5 V and a 3.3 V ESP32 data line is BELOW threshold -> unreliable/flickering first pixel or whole-strip garbage. The chosen mitigation drops strip Vdd via a 1N5819 to ~4.3 V (0.7*4.3=3.0 V), which only just clears 3.3 V and depends on the diode's actual forward drop at the strip's real current; it is not a guaranteed fix like a 74AHCT125.
findings.expected: A dedicated 74AHCT125/level shifter guarantees a clean 5 V-logic data edge.
findings.suspected: Diode-drop approach is chosen; may show intermittent first-LED corruption or strip glitches on real hardware, especially as strip current (and thus diode drop) varies.
findings.how_to_verify: On the bench (D8 Phase 9), drive the strip and scope the data line high level vs the strip's Vdd*0.7; watch for a flaky first pixel. If unreliable, fit the 74AHCT125 the docs already keep as the fallback.
findings.fix_direction: Keep the 74AHCT125 as the primary plan rather than the fallback, or verify the 1N5819 margin on the actual strip before committing.

findings.title: ESC and steering signal pins float high-Z for the boot window before setup() drives them
findings.evidence: setup() (main.cpp:167-184) is where escPwm.begin(neutralMicros) / steeringPwm.begin(centerMicros) first drive GPIO14/13. Before that, from power-on/reset until setup() runs, no code configures those pins and Esp32LedcPwm does nothing until begin(). No boot-time pinMode/pull is set on 13/14.
findings.why_matters: For the tens of ms between reset and setup(), GPIO14 (ESC signal) and GPIO13 (steering) are default inputs (high-Z). Most ESCs treat absent/invalid PWM as disarmed-neutral, and the servo holds, so this is normally harmless -- but it is an unproven assumption that depends on the specific QuicRun 10BL120 behavior and on no stray noise being interpreted as a pulse.
findings.expected: A defined safe level on ESC/servo signal lines from the instant of power-on.
findings.suspected: Brief indeterminate signal at boot; likely benign (ESC stays disarmed) but not guaranteed.
findings.how_to_verify: D8 Phase 3/7: scope GPIO14 from power-on and confirm the ESC does not twitch/arm before the firmware's neutral hold; confirm no steering-servo kick at power-on.
findings.fix_direction: Optionally add an external pulldown on the ESC signal line if the ESC misbehaves at boot; otherwise accept as ESC-dependent.

findings.title: Wheel-speed plausibility clamp (5000 rpm) and the Hall ISR lockout (2 ms) assume different 'top speeds'
findings.evidence: WheelSpeed.hpp:19 'maxPlausibleRpm = 5000' with comment '~55 rev/s at the car's real top speed' -- but 5000 rpm = 83.3 rev/s, not 55. Meanwhile Esp32HallPulseCounter.hpp lockout comment says 'Real pulses are >=18ms apart at the car's top speed' (=55 rev/s) justifying kLockoutUs=2000.
findings.why_matters: Two files encode two different 'top speeds' (83 rev/s vs 55 rev/s). Neither breaks function -- the 2 ms lockout (allows up to ~30000 rpm) is far looser than the 5000 rpm plausibility clamp, so the lockout does NOT actually defend the plausibility ceiling against EMI double-counts between 5000 and 30000 rpm-equivalent edges. It is a documentation/consistency gap that could mislead future tuning.
findings.expected: One coherent top-speed number and a lockout that meaningfully guards the plausibility clamp.
findings.suspected: Comment says 55 rev/s but clamp is set for 83 rev/s; lockout window is much looser than the clamp.
findings.how_to_verify: D8 Phase 8: scope the Hall line at full throttle near the motor and measure real minimum edge spacing and EMI, then reconcile maxPlausibleRpm and kLockoutUs to the measured top speed.
findings.fix_direction: Pick the real measured top speed, fix the misleading '55 rev/s' comment, and consider tightening kLockoutUs toward the plausibility-clamp period.

findings.title: WS2812 compile-time power budget only checks amber (2-channel); white halo (3-channel) is not the modeled worst case
findings.evidence: LightRenderer.hpp:60-61 'Estimate worst case: all pixels amber (R+G) at the cap. const uint32_t perLedMa = (2u * 20u * maxBrightness) / 255u;' -- only 2 channels. Comment line 56 asserts 'hazard (single amber color) is the real worst case,' but any white/teal-heavy frame lights all 3 channels.
findings.why_matters: 3-channel white at cap = ~776 mA for 30 px vs the 517 mA amber estimate. Both are under the 900 mA budget here, so no real risk at 30 px / brightness 110, but the valid() proof does not actually bound the true worst case; if numPixels or maxBrightness are raised later the check could pass while real draw exceeds budget.
findings.expected: The budget check should model the true worst case (3 channels) or justify why amber dominates.
findings.suspected: Under-modeled worst case; harmless at current constants, fragile under future changes.
findings.how_to_verify: Compute 3*20*maxBrightness/255 * kNumPixels and confirm <= kBudgetMilliamps for any future pixel/brightness change; measure strip current on the bench at a white halo frame.
findings.fix_direction: Change the perLedMa estimate to 3 channels (or document why amber is the true ceiling given the compositor's color set).

findings.title: ADC has no headroom margin if the 27k/10k divider tolerance or a slightly-over-8.4 V pack pushes the pin toward the 11 dB ceiling
findings.evidence: Esp32BatteryAdc.hpp:19 '11dB (0..~2450mV usable; 8.4V reads ~2270mV at the pin)'. Divider ideal 8.4V -> 2.27V. With 1% resistors the ratio can shift ~+/-2%, and a hot-off-charger 2S can momentarily read >8.4 V.
findings.why_matters: 2.27 V nominal leaves ~180 mV headroom to the ~2.45 V linear ceiling. Resistor tolerance plus a slightly high pack could push the pin into the ADC's non-linear top region, making the top-of-charge reading read low / compressed. Battery telemetry is monitoring-only (CLAUDE.md 6.4) so this cannot cause unsafe motion, but it degrades the accuracy exactly where it matters (full-charge reference for two-point cal).
findings.expected: Pin voltage stays comfortably in the ADC linear region across tolerance + full charge.
findings.suspected: Marginal headroom at full charge; possible compressed high-end reading.
findings.how_to_verify: D8 Phase 8 two-point cal: at ~8.4 V compare console mV to multimeter; if the high point reads low relative to the 6.5 V point, the pin is clipping the ADC ceiling.
findings.fix_direction: If the high end clips, nudge the divider (e.g. slightly larger high-side resistor) rather than only trimming calibrationPpt, since ppt cannot correct ADC non-linearity.

open_questions: Does the QuicRun 10BL120 treat a floating/absent PWM signal at ESP32 boot as disarmed-neutral, or can boot noise on GPIO14 be misread? (bench-only, D8 Phase 7)
Is the real minimum Hall edge spacing at full throttle closer to 12 ms (5000 rpm clamp) or 18 ms (ISR comment)? Determines whether the 2 ms lockout should be tightened.
Will the 1N5819 diode-drop reliably clear the WS2812 3.3 V logic-high threshold at the strip's real operating current, or is the 74AHCT125 needed?

The atlas footer states 'pin numbers are illustrative — your firmware defines the real ones,' so the atlas cannot serve as the pin-map authority CLAUDE.md section 1 implies; the effective authority is CLAUDE.md + BOM, which do agree with PinMap. Is that intended?

hw_validation_items.item: Scope GPIO14 (ESC signal) and GPIO13 (steering) from power-on to first setup() write; confirm no ESC arm/twitch and no servo kick during the float window.
Verify RP1 CRSF output is 420000 8N1 NOT inverted (D8 Phase 2) — the firmware assumes non-inverted and has no inversion path.
Confirm ESC throttle RED (+5V BEC) wire is physically isolated at ESP32 #1 before any battery is connected (BEC back-feed damages the ESC).
WS2812 data-line scope: measure logic-high vs strip Vdd*0.7 with the 1N5819 in place; fit 74AHCT125 if the first pixel is flaky.
Battery ADC two-point calibration at ~6.5 V and ~8.4 V; check the high point isn't compressed by the 11 dB ADC ceiling; log the eFuse cal type reported.
Hall line EMI check at full throttle near the brushless motor; add 1-10 nF across the sensor output if edges are ugly; reconcile maxPlausibleRpm/kLockoutUs to measured top speed.
Confirm shared common ground across battery, ESC, both BECs, both ESP32s, camera, WiFi module, RP1 (the cross-board UART 25->16 and CRSF both depend on it).

dimensions.summary: The control firmware is unusually disciplined for a hobby project: a fully non-blocking loop, a clean HAL seam, unsigned millis() deltas throughout, an atomic Hall ISR, and a genuinely layered safety chain (FailsafeStateMachine forces neutral -> ArmGate re-neutral latch -> EscOutput boot-arm hold -> multiplicative ERS boost that cannot bypass the gate). I traced the safety path end-to-end and it holds: at boot and on link loss the ESC is commanded neutral before any feature runs, and the arm gate force-disarms on Safe. That said, "guilty until proven on hardware" surfaces several real concerns. The most serious are hardware-gated and cannot be closed without bring-up: the LEDC 50 Hz PWM has never been scope-verified, the ESC arm-hold value is a guess against an unread ESC manual, and GPIO35's Hall edge/lockout is untested against real ESC EMI. In pure logic I found a small number of genuine defects (a WheelSpeed decay-ceiling division that can under-report a moving wheel to a near-stop reading, a one-cycle-stale FLIGHTMODE telemetry snapshot, and a torn WheelPulseSnapshot that is documented but can briefly report a wrong RPM). None are crash bugs; tests pass but several test the intended behavior rather than the corner cases below.
well_designed: Failsafe ordering is correct and cannot be bypassed: in loop() the Safe branch (main.cpp:340-348) unconditionally commands steering 0 / esc.setThrottle(0) / drs closed and sets armed=false, and ArmGate.update() is called every control tick with forceDisarm = (state==Safe) (main.cpp:318-319), which clears seenNeutralSinceEnable_ (ArmGate.cpp:8-10). ERS applyBoost is purely multiplicative (ErsSystem.cpp:56-66, applyBoost(0)==0), so boosting after the arm gate cannot resurrect throttle. This matches CLAUDE.md 6.1/6.2 exactly.

FailsafeStateMachine (FailsafeStateMachine.cpp) latches Safe until everReceivedFrame_ AND link fresh AND !rxFailsafeFlag, with immediate entry to Safe and a rearmConfirmMs=150 continuous-validity requirement to leave it. The everReceivedFrame_ latch (never inferring health from timestamps alone) directly fixes the documented boot-time full-lock bug (ROADMAP A1). rxSignalsFailsafe latches on uplink LQ==0 and clears only on LQ>0 (CrsfReceiver.hpp:44-46), a correct independent loss signal that survives hold-position RC frames.

The whole control path is non-blocking: loop() uses only nowMs-last>=period guards (main.cpp:251,260,303,377), no delay() anywhere in src/ or lib/. The tick guard is deliberately phase-accumulating (last=now, not last+=period, main.cpp:298-302) so a stall cannot cause a catch-up burst. EscOutput boot-arm is clock-injected and non-blocking (EscOutput.cpp:8-9), not a delay().

Hall ISR concurrency is done right for a 32-bit MCU: count_ and lastPeriodUs_ are std::atomic<uint32_t> with relaxed ordering (single-word, no torn read), the ISR is IRAM_ATTR (Esp32HallPulseCounter.cpp:17-32) so it survives flash-cache-disabled NVS writes, and lastEdgeUs_/edgeSeen_ are ISR-only. WheelSpeed seeds on first update to avoid a boot spike (WheelSpeed.cpp:11-18).

Every actuator output defensively clamps: ServoOutput.cpp:8-28 and EscOutput.cpp:23-42 clamp input to [-1000,1000] and then clamp the computed microseconds to [minMicros,maxMicros] regardless of trim; ServoConfig.valid() additionally rejects a trim that would push center past an endpoint (ServoOutput.hpp:18-22). LEDC begin() writes an explicit safe initial pulse so outputs never depend on call ordering (Esp32LedcPwm.hpp:18-23, main.cpp:180-184).

NVS settings have a real never-brick guard: deserialize() (Settings.cpp:29-47) requires exact length, CRC8 match, version match, AND candidate.valid() before ever applying; any failure falls back to kDefaults (ConsoleRunner.cpp:5-16). The tuning console is compile-gated (-DW17_TUNING_CONSOLE) so the delivered firmware opens no UART0 surface and cannot mutate live config.

Integer scaling is carefully overflow-analyzed: BatteryConfig.valid() (BatteryMonitor.hpp:38-41) statically proves 3300*dividerNum*calibrationPpt fits u32 and the result fits u16; gearbox expo keeps x^3<=1e9 in int32 with a comment (Gearbox.cpp:21); ERS stores energy in micro-permille so per-tick drain=rate*dtMs needs no division and cannot truncate (ErsSystem.hpp:78-83) with a kMaxDtMs stall guard.

findings.title: LEDC 50 Hz servo/ESC PWM has never been scope-verified; 16-bit resolution at 50 Hz is at/near the ESP32 LEDC limit and duty math is untested on hardware
findings.evidence: Esp32LedcPwm.hpp:28-31 sets kFrequencyHz=50, kResolutionBits=16, kMaxDuty=65535; setPulseMicroseconds does duty=(microseconds*65535)/20000 (Esp32LedcPwm.cpp:15-18). No hardware or Wokwi scope confirmation of actual pulse width exists.
findings.why_matters: Every actuator (steering, ESC throttle, DRS, gimbal) depends on this producing correct 1000-2000us pulses at a stable 50Hz. If the LEDC timer cannot achieve 16-bit resolution at 50Hz on this core clock, ledcSetup silently reduces resolution and every pulse width is wrong -> ESC mis-arms or servos peg. This is the single most load-bearing untested conversion in the firmware.
findings.expected: 1500us commands a 1500us pulse (+-a few us) at 50Hz measured on a scope; 1000/2000us endpoints correct.
findings.suspected: Untested on real hardware. 1500us -> duty 4915/65535; plausibly correct, but LEDC resolution-vs-frequency limits and ledcSetup's silent clamp are unverified.
findings.how_to_verify: Scope GPIO13/14/18 on a real ESP32-WROOM-32; confirm 50Hz period and 1000/1500/2000us pulses. Check ledcSetup return value / actual resolution at 50Hz/16-bit.
findings.fix_direction: On the bench, verify pulse widths with a scope/logic analyzer before connecting the ESC or servos; if resolution is clamped, drop to 14-bit and rescale kMaxDuty.

findings.title: ESC boot-arm hold (bootArmHoldMs=2000) is a guess not reconciled against the Hobbywing QuicRun 10BL120 arming spec
findings.evidence: EscConfig.bootArmHoldMs default 2000 (EscOutput.hpp:14-17) with comment 'most ESCs require this to arm'; no citation to the QuicRun 10BL120 manual, which is not in the repo docs.
findings.why_matters: If the real ESC needs a longer neutral hold, a different arm tone/sequence, or throttle-endpoint calibration on first power-up, the ESC may fail to arm (car dead) or, worse, mis-calibrate its endpoints from whatever pulse it sees. The firmware's neutral-hold is correct in structure but the value/behavior is unvalidated against the actual ESC.
findings.expected: Neutral held long enough that the QuicRun 10BL120 arms reliably every boot, matching its documented arming procedure.
findings.suspected: 2000ms may be too short/long, and the ESC may expect a calibration gesture the firmware never performs.
findings.how_to_verify: Read the QuicRun 10BL120 manual arming spec; power the ESC from the firmware and confirm it arms every time across several boots.
findings.fix_direction: Confirm the ESC's arming/neutral requirement from its manual, add it to docs, and set bootArmHoldMs accordingly; verify on the bench with the motor disconnected first.

findings.title: WheelSpeed graceful-decay ceiling uses integer division that collapses to near-zero one tick before the timeout, under-reporting a still-moving wheel
findings.evidence: WheelSpeed.cpp:48-54: impliedCeilingRpm = 60000u / (elapsedMs * magnetsPerRev); with magnetsPerRev=1 and elapsedMs approaching zeroSpeedTimeoutMs=1500, e.g. elapsedMs=1000 gives ceiling=60 rpm, elapsedMs=1499 gives ceiling=40 rpm, and the report is clamped down to that even if the wheel is truly turning at, say, 200 rpm but the last magnet pass was slightly delayed.
findings.why_matters: With a single axle magnet (magnetsPerRev=1, CLAUDE.md 7), at low RPM the pulse interval is long, so between real pulses the decay ceiling drops the reported RPM/speed far below actual. The RPM feeds the sound board engine note (link2) and the GPS groundspeed telemetry to the ground station, so both will show the car sagging toward stop while it is still rolling. This is a fidelity bug, not a safety bug, but it directly contradicts the header's promise of graceful decay.
findings.expected: Reported RPM decays smoothly and only reaches ~0 near the real stop; a wheel turning at a steady low speed reads roughly steady between pulses.
findings.suspected: With one magnet, reported speed visibly droops between every pulse at low speed and steps back up on each pulse, producing a saw-tooth that reads much lower than actual most of the time.
findings.how_to_verify: Native test: seed a period corresponding to 120 rpm (1 magnet -> 500ms period), then call update() at 20ms cadence for 480ms with no new pulse and assert reportedRpm stays near 120 rather than collapsing toward the elapsed-based ceiling.
findings.fix_direction: Only apply the elapsed-based ceiling once elapsedMs exceeds the last measured period (you cannot have missed a pulse until you are past due), rather than from the first silent tick; or add a second magnet to raise the pulse rate.

findings.title: Hall GPIO35 rising-edge detection and 2ms lockout are unverified against real ESC/brushless EMI on a slow RC edge
findings.evidence: onEdge() uses a 2000us lockout (Esp32HallPulseCounter.hpp:36) justified by an assumed >=18ms real pulse spacing at top speed; the header itself warns GPIO35 has no Schmitt trigger and the 10k-pullup edge is slow so 'ESC EMI riding a slow edge can double-count' (Esp32HallPulseCounter.hpp:16-21). No hardware test exists.
findings.why_matters: If EMI produces bursts >2ms apart, the 2ms lockout will not reject them and the counter over-counts, inflating RPM/speed telemetry and the engine-sound pitch. Conversely the maxPlausibleRpm=5000 clamp (WheelSpeed) hides gross over-counts but not moderate ones. Whether 2ms is adequate is a physical-EMI question that only bench testing under motor load can answer.
findings.expected: Clean one-pulse-per-magnet counting with the motor running under load.
findings.suspected: Possible double/multi counting from ESC switching noise on the slow, Schmitt-less GPIO35 edge; lockout value may need tuning or an RC filter/Schmitt buffer added.
findings.how_to_verify: On the bench with the motor spinning under ESC drive, log raw edge count vs known wheel revolutions; scope GPIO35 for noise/ringing on each magnet pass.
findings.fix_direction: If double-counts appear, add a hardware RC low-pass or Schmitt buffer on GPIO35 and/or retune kLockoutUs; consider a min-period plausibility gate tied to maxPlausibleRpm.

findings.title: FLIGHTMODE telemetry string reads controlSnapshot fields (displayGear, driveMode, ersPercent) that are only refreshed in the 20 Hz link2 block, so the up-linked status can be one gear/mode behind
findings.evidence: The 5 Hz telemetry block builds 'G%u M%u E%u' from controlSnapshot.displayGear/driveMode/ersPercent (main.cpp:287-290), but those three fields are assigned only inside the 20 Hz link2 block (main.cpp:381,384,386). displayGear and driveMode are never set in the 50 Hz control tick, so at the instant the telemetry block runs they reflect the previous link2 send.
findings.why_matters: The FLIGHTMODE frame is described as the car-authoritative source the HUD prefers over its own mirror (main.cpp:282-285). A up to ~50ms stale gear/mode/ERS in that string is cosmetic, but it means the 'authoritative' value can momentarily disagree with the link2 value sent to the sound board, and right after boot (before the first link2 tick) it reports the boot defaults G1 M1 E100.
findings.expected: The up-linked gear/mode/ERS reflect the current control state at send time.
findings.suspected: Values lag by up to one link2 period (50ms) and show boot defaults until the first 20Hz tick.
findings.how_to_verify: Trace field writes: displayGear/driveMode assigned only at main.cpp:381/386 (20Hz), read at main.cpp:288-290 (5Hz). Confirm no writer in the 50Hz block.
findings.fix_direction: Compute displayGear/driveMode/ersPercent from the live modules (virtualGearbox.currentGear()+1, controls.driveMode, ersSystem.energyPercent()) directly in the telemetry block, or update the snapshot in the control tick.

findings.title: WheelPulseSnapshot count/period can be torn (count from a newer edge than period), briefly reporting a wrong RPM after a sudden speed change
findings.evidence: read() loads count_ and lastPeriodUs_ as two separate relaxed atomic reads (Esp32HallPulseCounter.cpp:34-37); the ISR updates period then count (Esp32HallPulseCounter.cpp:27-31). The header explicitly accepts this: 'The pair may rarely be torn (count from a newer edge than the period)' (IWheelPulseSensor.hpp:11-14).
findings.why_matters: WheelSpeed treats a changed count as 'new pulse' and recomputes RPM from lastPeriodMicros (WheelSpeed.cpp:20-35). If the count increment is seen but the period store from that same edge is not yet visible, one update uses the previous period -> one wrong RPM sample. Documented and clamped, so impact is a single-sample telemetry glitch, not a control fault.
findings.expected: count and period always come from the same edge.
findings.suspected: Rare single-sample RPM error on abrupt speed change (accepted by design).
findings.how_to_verify: Code review confirms the two-load tear window; magnitude is one control tick (20ms).
findings.fix_direction: If it ever matters, pack count+period into a single 64-bit atomic or use a seqlock; otherwise document as accepted (already is).

findings.title: No explicit loop/task watchdog handling; reliance on Arduino-ESP32 default WDT config is unverified for this build
findings.evidence: No esp_task_wdt_* calls, no yield(), and no WDT-related build flags anywhere (grep of src/, lib/, platformio.ini returned nothing). The loop() drains the CRSF UART in a while(available()) loop (main.cpp:215-219) with no explicit feed.
findings.why_matters: The Arduino loopTask normally is not subscribed to the task WDT by default, and the loop is non-blocking, so this is probably fine. But there is no defense-in-depth: if a future change or a stuck UART ever spun the drain loop, nothing would reset the MCU. For a car that can drive away, a watchdog is cheap insurance and its absence is worth a deliberate decision.
findings.expected: A deliberate WDT policy (either the loop is WDT-monitored or explicitly not, documented).
findings.suspected: Default behavior relied upon implicitly; not validated on hardware.
findings.how_to_verify: On hardware, deliberately block the loop and observe whether the chip resets; check the effective sdkconfig for CONFIG_ESP_TASK_WDT and loop-task subscription.
findings.fix_direction: Decide and document the WDT policy; optionally enable the task WDT on the loop task and feed it once per loop.

findings.title: Camera gimbal pan/tilt servos are driven from controls.pan/tilt even in Training/plain modes and are never re-centered on failsafe, holding last position during an outage
findings.evidence: panServo/tiltServo.setPosition(controls.pan/tilt) run only in the non-Safe branch (main.cpp:372-373); during Safe the block is skipped so the gimbal holds its last commanded position (documented at main.cpp:367-371). controls is frozen during an outage since decode is event-driven.
findings.why_matters: Purely cosmetic/UX, but worth flagging: on link loss the camera does not return to a known center, and there is no mode gating, so any noise on ch9/ch10 (or an absent channel decoding to 0 = center) moves the servos. Not a safety issue (aiming a camera is harmless), but MG90S on GPIO19/23 will keep drawing holding current at whatever angle.
findings.expected: Defined gimbal behavior on failsafe (hold vs center) — intentionally 'hold' here.
findings.suspected: Behavior is intentional per the comment; flagged only so the reviewer/owner confirms 'hold last look' is desired rather than center-on-failsafe.
findings.how_to_verify: Confirm with owner that camera-hold-on-failsafe is intended; no code defect.
findings.fix_direction: None required; if center-on-failsafe is preferred, add pan/tilt setPosition(0) to the Safe branch.

open_questions: Does the Hobbywing QuicRun 10BL120 arm with a plain 2000ms neutral hold, or does it require throttle-endpoint calibration / a specific arming gesture on first power-up? The manual is not in the repo.
Can the ESP32 LEDC achieve true 16-bit resolution at 50 Hz on this board's clock, or does ledcSetup silently clamp resolution (which would corrupt every pulse width)? Needs scope confirmation.
Is 2 ms an adequate Hall lockout against real ESC EMI on the Schmitt-less GPIO35 input under motor load, or is a hardware RC filter / Schmitt buffer needed?

CRSF telemetry frames the control board sends UP (battery/GPS/flightmode) all use sync byte 0xC8 in buildFrame; is 0xC8 the correct address byte for device->RX telemetry on this RP1/ELRS setup, or should it be a device address like 0xEC? (Cross-check with ground-station decode dimension.)
Is the Arduino loopTask subscribed to the task WDT in this exact platform/framework version, and is that the intended policy?

hw_validation_items.item: Scope GPIO13/14/18 pulse widths (1000/1500/2000us) and period (50Hz) on a real ESP32 before connecting ESC/servos; confirm LEDC did not silently reduce resolution at 16-bit/50Hz.
Confirm the ESC arms every boot with the firmware's neutral-hold sequence (motor disconnected first), and reconcile bootArmHoldMs against the QuicRun 10BL120 manual.

Verify the full safety chain on the bench: power up with no CRSF (expect ESC neutral, DRS closed, steering center), then bring the link up mid-throttle and confirm the motor stays off until the stick returns to neutral (ArmGate) and the rearm window elapses.

With the motor spinning under ESC drive, log raw Hall edge count vs known wheel revolutions and scope GPIO35 for EMI/ringing; retune kLockoutUs or add an RC/Schmitt front-end if double-counting appears.
Calibrate the battery divider with a multimeter and set calibrationPpt; confirm the low-voltage warning latches at ~7.0V under load and clears with hysteresis.
Confirm CRSF RX at 420000 8N1 non-inverted on GPIO16 actually parses RC frames from the RP1 (link comes up, channels move), since baud/inversion is untested on hardware.
Verify low-speed wheel-speed reporting on a rolling bench (single magnet) does not saw-tooth toward zero between pulses (WheelSpeed decay-ceiling finding); consider a second magnet.

dimensions.summary: The core-2 cross-core design is genuinely sound: the only shared mutable surface between the core-1 control loop and the core-0 audio task is the single std::atomic<uint32_t> packed-param word plus a std::atomic<uint32_t> heartbeat, and the EngineSynth object is touched exclusively by the audio task, so there is no torn read, no shared VehicleState, and no lock. The audio dead-man (heartbeat stale >500ms -> forced silent params, ramped to 0 by the smoother) is implemented and correct, including at boot (heartbeat=0 yields silent params for the first 500ms). link2monitor implements the mandated 500ms staleness -> local failsafe with a well-reasoned per-field projection, and the frame assembler hard-rejects unsupported length bytes the instant they arrive (State::ReadingLength). Integer DSP has an explicit clip clamp and a compile-time headroom check, and the integration + synth tests demonstrate no clipping across drive scripts. The prompt's worry that enginesim treats WHEEL rpm as engine rpm is NOT borne out: enginesim derives engine rpm purely from throttlePercent and never reads state.rpm at all -- which is defensible but means the decoded wheel-rpm field is entirely dead on this board. The main real defects are lower-severity: a NeverConnected-forever failure mode that shows a calm "waiting" animation instead of any fault indication when board #1 is dead at power-on, a production volume-mapping formula in main.cpp (volumeFor) that no test actually exercises (tests use a different formula), and the overrun noise burst (noiseAmpMax*3) not being accounted for in the compile-time headroom check (it survives only by the runtime clamp).
well_designed: Cross-core contract is exactly as CLAUDE.md claims and is airtight: src/main.cpp:23-26 declares the only two shared objects as std::atomic<uint32_t> gSynthParams and gControlHeartbeatMs. The control loop (core 1) only .store()s these (main.cpp:125-129); the audio task (core 0) only .load()s them (main.cpp:70,74) and exclusively owns the EngineSynth object. No VehicleState, no synth phase, and no EngineState ever crosses cores. A 32-bit atomic is genuinely torn-free on ESP32, so this is a correct lock-free hand-off.

Audio dead-man is real and correct (main.cpp:69-75): each audio iteration reads the heartbeat and, if now-hb > 500ms, calls synth.setParams(0,0,...) forcing silence regardless of the last packed word. Combined with the render() smoother (EngineSynth.cpp:83 smoothVolume ramps toward 0) this ramps to silence rather than cutting hard. At boot heartbeat=0 so the first 500ms also renders the silent packed word (gSynthParams default 0), so a wedged or never-started control loop cannot scream.

Mandatory 500ms link staleness -> local failsafe is implemented (Link2Monitor.cpp:31,39-54) with a thoughtful per-field projection: commands (throttle/steer/armed/drs/ers) and rpm are forced safe/zero while latched judgments (lowBattery, batteryMv, gear, ersPercent, driveMode) are held, and gear is deliberately held to avoid a phantom shift-blip on recovery. Boot state is NeverConnected + failsafe=true (Link2Monitor.cpp:8, VehicleState default failsafe=true). Well covered by test_link2monitor.

Length byte is hard-rejected immediately, not buffered: Link2FrameAssembler::feedByte State::ReadingLength (Link2Codec.cpp:90-97) returns FrameInvalid and resyncs to WaitingForStart the moment a non-11 length arrives, exactly as the protocol comment (Link2Frame.hpp:11-14) and assembler comment (Link2Codec.hpp:24-27) require -- a corrupt 0xFF length cannot swallow ~1s of following frames.

enginesim does NOT confuse wheel rpm with engine rpm: EngineSim::targetRpm (EngineSim.cpp:14-21) maps throttlePercent 0..100 across idleRpm..maxRpm and the wheel-rpm field state.rpm is never referenced anywhere in enginesim (grep confirms only Link2Codec and Link2Monitor touch .rpm). The engine note therefore tracks commanded throttle, matching the protocol's stated intent (Link2Frame.hpp:18-21).

Clip safety is layered: EngineSynthConfig::valid() (EngineSynth.hpp:69-73) is a compile-time static_assert (main.cpp:33-34) that peakSum() (partials+noise+whine) stays <= kHeadroomPeak=30000, and render() has a final hard clamp to int16 (EngineSynth.cpp:141-142). test_never_clips_at_max_settings and the integration test's test_never_clips_across_a_full_drive_script both verify no overflow at max settings.

Lights failsafe/hazard precedence is correct and tested: LightRenderer::render computes localFailsafe = state.failsafe || link==Lost (LightRenderer.cpp:63) and short-circuits to all-amber hazard blink before any functional layer (LightRenderer.cpp:84-90). Power budget is enforced at compile time via LightConfig::valid() worst-case all-amber current estimate (LightRenderer.hpp:59-66, static_assert main.cpp:35-36); test_config_valid_and_within_power_budget confirms maxBrightness=255 is rejected.

Per-sample param smoothing (EngineSynth.cpp:82-83, >>6 one-pole toward target) removes zipper/click on the 50Hz param steps, and the phase-accumulator wavetable with milli-Hz phase increments (EngineSynth.cpp:40-45, 64-bit mul then divide) keeps pitch precise at low rpm. Deterministic xorshift noise makes render() reproducible and unit-testable (test_deterministic_given_seed).

findings.title: Dead board #1 at power-on stays NeverConnected forever showing a calm breathe, never escalating to a fault indication
findings.evidence: LightRenderer.cpp:70-81: if (neverConnected) renders only the calm teal 'waiting breathe' on the halo and returns. Link2Monitor.cpp:24-28: while !everReceived_, status stays NeverConnected regardless of elapsed time; the comment LightRenderer.cpp:67-69 explicitly assumes 'a genuine cut wire reads as Lost -> hazard', which only holds if at least one valid frame arrived first.
findings.why_matters: If board #1 is dead, unplugged, or the link2 wire is open at power-on (never sends a valid frame), the sound/light board shows a gentle breathing animation indefinitely with no hazard/fault signal. The engine side is safe (VehicleState default armed=false -> Ignition::Off), so this is diagnostic/UX, not unsafe motion, but it masks a real bring-up fault exactly when the builder needs to see it.
findings.expected: After some bootstrap window with no first frame ever, escalate NeverConnected to a distinct fault indication (e.g. hazard or a red waiting state), not a calm breathe that is indistinguishable from normal boot.
findings.suspected: NeverConnected renders a calm breathe forever; only Lost (post-first-frame) shows hazard.
findings.how_to_verify: On the bench, power ESP32 #2 with the link2 RX wire disconnected and confirm it breathes teal indefinitely instead of indicating a fault.
findings.fix_direction: Add a NeverConnected timeout in the monitor or renderer: after N seconds with everReceived_==false, treat as a fault (hazard or amber). Keep engine silent as-is.

findings.title: Production volume mapping in main.cpp volumeFor is untested; tests exercise a different formula
findings.evidence: main.cpp:56-60 volumeFor: Cranking->70, Running-> 90 + throttle*165/100 (90..255). The integration test defines its OWN volumeFor (test/test_integration/*.cpp): Cranking->60, Running-> 80 + throttle*175/100 (80..255). No native test includes or calls the main.cpp volumeFor; main.cpp is excluded from the native build (platformio.ini test_build_src = no).
findings.why_matters: The actual on-car volume curve is never validated. A regression or typo in the shipped volumeFor (e.g. an overflow or wrong slope) would pass all 40 tests. The two formulas already diverge, so 'tests confirm loudness behavior' is a false assurance for the real mapping.
findings.expected: The production volume mapping is the one under test.
findings.suspected: Two divergent volumeFor definitions; the tested one is a copy that can drift from the shipped one.
findings.how_to_verify: Grep for volumeFor: it is defined independently in main.cpp and in test_integration with different constants.
findings.fix_direction: Move volumeFor into a pure lib (e.g. enginesim or a small mapping header) and call it from both main.cpp and the test, so the shipped mapping is the tested mapping.

findings.title: Overrun crackle noise burst (noiseAmpMax*3) is not accounted for in the compile-time headroom check
findings.evidence: EngineSynth.cpp:110-112 sets noiseAmp = config_.noiseAmpMax*3 (=4800 for the default 1600) during overrun bursts, but EngineSynthConfig::peakSum() (EngineSynth.hpp:60-67) only sums noiseAmpMax (1600), not 3x. Default worst case with the burst: 20200(partials)+4800(noise)+2800(whine)=27800, still < kHeadroomPeak=30000, so it happens to be safe today.
findings.why_matters: The static_assert(kSynthConfig.valid()) advertises clip-proofness, but the headroom accounting understates the real peak by 2*noiseAmpMax. If a future tune raises noiseAmpMax or partial weights, valid() could pass while overrun bursts clip (only the runtime clamp at EngineSynth.cpp:141-142 saves it, at the cost of audible hard clipping).
findings.expected: peakSum() reflects the true worst-case instantaneous sum including the 3x overrun burst.
findings.suspected: valid()/peakSum() budgets 1x noiseAmpMax but render() can emit 3x during overrun.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare EngineSynth.cpp:111 (noiseAmpMax*3) against EngineSynth.hpp:65 (peakSum adds noiseAmpMax once).
findings.fix_direction: Use 3*noiseAmpMax in peakSum() (or cap the overrun burst to fit the existing budget) so the compile-time guard matches the runtime worst case.

findings.title: Decoded wheel-rpm field is dead on board #2; drivemode/gear label mismatch persists downstream
findings.evidence: Link2Codec.cpp:72 decodes out.rpm, Link2Monitor.cpp:52 zeroes it on staleness, but no consumer reads state.rpm (grep across lib/ and src/ shows only codec + monitor touch .rpm). driveMode is decoded (Link2Codec.cpp:75) and used only for the rain light (LightRenderer.cpp:113 checks driveMode==2), matching the Frame.hpp label '2=Gearbox+ERS' but the ground-station labels the same value differently (established fact).
findings.why_matters: The wheel-rpm telemetry that board #1 spends bytes sending is never used on board #2 (engine note is throttle-derived), so any assumption that the sound tracks measured ground speed is wrong. Harmless today but a latent confusion for anyone tuning the engine feel or reusing the frame.
findings.expected: Either wheel rpm feeds something (e.g. a speed-scaled tire/road noise) or it is documented as unused on this board.
findings.suspected: wheel rpm decoded and zeroed-on-stale but otherwise ignored.
findings.how_to_verify: grep -rn state.rpm lib/ src/ -- only codec/monitor appear.
findings.fix_direction: Document that engine rpm is throttle-derived and wheel rpm is unused on board #2, or wire wheel rpm into a road/tire-noise term if desired.

findings.title: NeoPixel show() runs on core 1 and briefly disables interrupts; verify it does not disturb link2 UART RX under load
findings.evidence: main.cpp:140 strip.show() (Adafruit_NeoPixel via RMT/bit-bang) runs from loop() on core 1, the same core as the interrupt-driven Serial2 RX drain (main.cpp:103-105). Adafruit NeoPixel's show() can disable interrupts for the duration of the transmit (~1ms for 30 GRB pixels at 800kHz on the bit-bang path). Audio I2S DMA is on core 0 and is unaffected.
findings.why_matters: At 115200 baud (~11.5 bytes/ms) a ~1ms interrupt disable is within the ESP32 UART hardware FIFO (128 bytes) so bytes should not be lost, but this is timing-dependent and unverified on hardware; the risk is occasional dropped link2 bytes causing extra frame resyncs (which are recoverable via the assembler).
findings.expected: No link2 byte loss during LED refresh.
findings.suspected: Possible brief UART ISR latency during show(); FIFO likely absorbs it.
findings.how_to_verify: On hardware, run link2 at full rate with LEDs animating and count Link2FrameAssembler FrameInvalid resyncs; confirm no sustained frame loss.
findings.fix_direction: If drops appear, confirm the ESP32 RMT (not bit-bang) NeoPixel path is used (it does not block interrupts) or move UART RX handling so it is not starved during show().

findings.title: i2s.begin() return codes are ignored; a failed driver install leaves write() blocking on portMAX_DELAY forever
findings.evidence: Esp32I2sAudio.cpp:38-39 i2s_driver_install/i2s_set_pin return esp_err_t but the results are discarded; write() (Esp32I2sAudio.cpp:46) uses portMAX_DELAY. If install fails (bad pin, resource conflict), the audio task blocks in i2s_write indefinitely.
findings.why_matters: A silent I2S init failure gives no diagnostic and wedges the audio task. Because the audio task only produces sound (no safety role) it will not affect motion, but it makes bring-up debugging harder (no sound, no error).
findings.expected: A failed I2S install is logged and the audio task degrades gracefully instead of blocking forever.
findings.suspected: return codes ignored; portMAX_DELAY blocks on a broken driver.
