findings.how_to_verify: Inspect Esp32I2sAudio::begin/write; no esp_err_t is checked.
findings.fix_direction: Check the esp_err_t from i2s_driver_install/i2s_set_pin, Serial-log on failure, and consider a bounded write timeout so the task can retry/report.

open_questions: Does the Arduino-ESP32 2.0.x Adafruit_NeoPixel path on this board use RMT (no interrupt disable) or the bit-bang fallback? The header comment (Esp32NeoPixelStrip.hpp:9-10) claims RMT; if so the interrupt-disable concern is moot. Needs hardware confirmation.

platformio.ini pins espressif32 @ ~7.0.1 but the audio HAL depends on the IDF 4.4 LEGACY i2s driver (driver/i2s.h). espressif32 7.x ships Arduino core 3.x / IDF 5.x where driver/i2s.h is deprecated (compat shim) or removed. The baseline says esp32dev builds, so it compiles today, but confirm the legacy i2s shim is actually present and functional under platform 7.0.1 on real hardware -- this contradicts the HAL header comment (Esp32I2sAudio.hpp:8-9) that claims Arduino core 2.0.x/IDF 4.4.

Is 22050 Hz / 256-frame / 6-DMA-buffer (~70ms latency) sufficient to avoid underrun given the audio task priority 5 and core-0 also servicing WiFi-less system tasks? render() is O(partials*frames) integer work and should be well under real-time, but not measured on hardware.

hw_validation_items.item: Power board #2 with the link2 RX wire disconnected at boot; confirm behavior (currently a calm breathe forever) is acceptable or shows a fault
Confirm I2S actually produces audio under platform espressif32 @ ~7.0.1 (legacy driver/i2s.h path) and does not fail-silent/block; check i2s init return codes on scope/serial
Run link2 at full 20Hz+ with LEDs animating and instrument Link2FrameAssembler FrameInvalid/resync count to confirm show() does not starve UART RX
Verify no audio underrun/glitch during sustained max-rpm + overrun + limiter rendering with lights active (audio task on core 0)
Confirm the real speaker/amp does not audibly clip during overrun crackle bursts (noiseAmpMax*3) at full volume

dimensions.summary: The ground station is genuinely viewer-only: there is no code path that opens the control TX port to write, the preload exposes only getConfig + onTelemetry (read), and the renderer is sandboxed with contextIsolation. The CRSF decode is a faithful, constant-for-constant port of the firmware wire format, and I verified byte-for-byte that the JS decoders for BATTERY (0x08), GPS (0x02, groundspeed offset 8-9), FLIGHTMODE (0x21 "G<n> M<n> E<n>"), and LINK_STATISTICS (0x14) match the firmware CrsfFrameBuilder and the values main.cpp actually emits. The real problems are behavioral/UX, not wire-format: (1) the HUD's "LINK LOST" alarm keys off telem.failsafe, but the only real telemetry source (crsf-serial) NEVER populates failsafe or armed — the firmware does not transmit those over CRSF — so a real car-side failsafe or ELRS link drop is invisible/misleading on the HUD, while the scripted demo shows a "LINK LOST" the real path can never produce. (2) Documented label/count mismatches persist (driveMode 1 shown as "RACE" vs firmware "Gearbox"; HUD sim gears=8 vs firmware 4). (3) The default telemetry source is 'none', so a fresh launch silently shows a fully animated fake HUD with no "this is simulated" alarm beyond a small "km/h · sim" tag. As a bring-up diagnostic the app is thin: it surfaces battery/speed/LQ if you win the COM-port fight, but has no raw-frame/decode-error/link-statistics debug view.
well_designed: Truly viewer-only, verified: preload.cjs exposes only getConfig (invoke) + onTelemetry (on) — no write/command channel exists; the renderer cannot reach ipcRenderer/require. main.js webPreferences sets contextIsolation:true, nodeIntegration:false, sandbox:true. index.html gate text and hud.js header comment both state the window mirrors inputs and does not command the car.

CRSF decode is a faithful port with matching constants (shared/crsf.js): SYNC 0xC8, CRC8 poly 0xD5 MSB-first init 0, payload lengths BATTERY=8, GPS=15, LINK_STATISTICS=10 — all identical to lib/crsf/include/crsf/CrsfFrame.hpp. decodeFrame's CRC is computed over [type+payload] exactly like the firmware.

Byte layouts verified against what the firmware actually emits: decodeGps reads groundspeed at payload[8..9]/10 and altitude at [12..13]-1000 — matches buildGpsFrame ordering in CrsfFrameBuilder.hpp and the speedKmhX10 the firmware computes in main.cpp:275. decodeBattery big-endian decivolts/deciamps/uint24/pct matches buildBatteryFrame. parseFlightMode's tolerant G/M/E regex matches main.cpp:287 snprintf("G%u M%u E%u").

CrsfSerialSource correctly accumulates a merged snapshot (this._telem + Object.assign) rather than replacing, so a battery frame does not blank speed/gear that arrive in separate frame types — the comment at lines 28-33 shows the author understood the partial-frame merge hazard.

serialport is an optionalDependency and lazy-required inside a try/catch (CrsfSerialSource._open lines 57-65) so the app degrades gracefully to a gamepad-only HUD if the native module isn't rebuilt; electron-builder.yml correctly asarUnpacks serialport and @serialport for ABI-native loading.

Robust child-process/socket lifecycle: MediamtxSupervisor spawns, restarts-on-crash with a _stopping guard, and is torn down on app will-quit (main.js:103-106); WHEP client auto-retries on failed/disconnected/closed with a single-timer guard and sets playoutDelayHint=0 for low-latency FPV.

scripts/run.js strips ELECTRON_RUN_AS_NODE (the VS Code integrated-terminal trap that boots Electron as bare Node) and scripts/ensure-electron.js repairs a half-installed Electron binary — real, correctly-diagnosed environment hazards for a Windows gift target.

Honest, well-reasoned docs: SETUP.md flags the H.265-vs-H.264 WebRTC risk as highest-priority bench item and gives a VLC zero-code fallback; TELEMETRY.md correctly explains the exclusive-COM-port obstacle (CreateFile dwShareMode=0) and the com0com/forward workarounds; CODESIGNING.md accurately states self-signed does not create trust without importing to the recipient's root store.

findings.title: HUD 'LINK LOST' alarm can never fire from the real telemetry source; only the fake demo shows it
findings.evidence: hud.js:206 `if (telem.failsafe) { linkEl.textContent = 'LINK LOST'; ... }`. But CrsfSerialSource/frameToTelemetry never set `failsafe` or `armed`: shared/crsfTelemetry.js frameToTelemetry maps only battery->{batteryV,batteryPct}, 0x14->{linkQualityPct}, 0x02->{speedKmh}, 0x21->{gear,driveMode,ersPct}. The firmware (src/main.cpp:256-294) transmits only BATTERY, GPS, FLIGHTMODE over CRSF — armed/failsafe are set only on the local link2 UART snapshot (controlSnapshot.failsafe at main.cpp:348), never up the ELRS backchannel.
findings.why_matters: The single most safety-relevant HUD indicator (LINK LOST) is dead in real operation. When the car actually enters failsafe or the ELRS uplink drops (LINK_STATISTICS uplinkLinkQuality->0), the HUD stays in the green 'live/LQ x%' branch (hud.js:207) or, if telemetry stops entirely for >1s, silently reverts to 'Telemetry: sim' and resumes animating a fake car. The owner sees a plausible HUD during an actual link loss. The demo/replay timeline (replaySource.js:18) scripts a failsafe+LINK LOST episode, so the feature looks tested/working but the real path can never reproduce it.
findings.expected: When uplinkLinkQuality hits 0 (ELRS-signalled loss) or telemetry goes stale, the HUD should raise the LINK LOST state; and/or the firmware should transmit an armed/failsafe indication and the source should map it.
findings.suspected: failsafe/armed are undefined on every real-source Telemetry object; LINK LOST only appears in scripted replay.
findings.how_to_verify: Run W17_TELEMETRY_SOURCE=crsf-serial, feed a LINK_STATISTICS frame with uplinkLinkQuality=0; observe hud.js shows 'LQ 0%' class 'link live', not 'LINK LOST'. Stop feeding frames >1s; observe it flips to 'Telemetry: sim' not an alarm.
findings.fix_direction: Either derive failsafe in frameToTelemetry from linkQualityPct==0 (map 0x14 LQ==0 -> failsafe:true), or add a firmware failsafe/armed telemetry field; and make >1s telemetry staleness a visible warning rather than a silent revert to sim.

findings.title: Default launch shows a fully-simulated HUD that can be mistaken for real car data
findings.evidence: main.js:33 `const kind = process.env.W17_TELEMETRY_SOURCE || 'none';` -> chooseTelemetrySource returns null. hud.js:158 telemetryLive() is false, so speed/rpm/gear/ers all come from updateSim() (hud.js:145) which animates a plausible 320 km/h F1 physics model. The only 'this is fake' cue is a small suffix 'km/h · sim' (hud.js:166) and 'Telemetry: sim' (hud.js:209). `npm start` runs with no env set, so the normal launch is the fully-simulated one.
findings.why_matters: For a bring-up tool, the default state should not look like a working car. speedbox shows large animated numbers; gear/rev-lights/ERS all move from the gamepad alone. During real bring-up the owner could believe the car is reporting speed/gear when nothing is connected. The distinction rests on a tiny text suffix.
findings.expected: A prominent, unmistakable 'NO TELEMETRY / SIMULATED' banner when hasTelemetrySource is false or telemetry is stale.
findings.suspected: Simulated physics is visually indistinguishable from live data except for a small tag.
findings.how_to_verify: Run `npm start` (no env). HUD animates speed/gear/ERS with only 'km/h · sim' as the cue.
findings.fix_direction: Make the sim state loud (dim/desaturate the real-only fields, banner). config:get already reports hasTelemetrySource (main.js:64) but the renderer ignores it — use it.

findings.title: driveMode label mismatch: HUD shows 'RACE'/'ERS' where firmware means 'Gearbox'/'Gearbox+ERS'
findings.evidence: hud.js:24-28 DRIVE_MODES = [{label:'TRAINING'},{label:'RACE'},{label:'ERS'}], indexed by telem.driveMode. Firmware lib/channels/include/channels/ChannelDecoder.hpp:24,64 documents driveMode 0=Training,1=Gearbox,2=Gearbox+ERS, and main.cpp:287 packs that raw number into 'M%u'. docs/TELEMETRY.md:16 also says '0=Training 1=Race 2=ERS' (agrees with HUD, disagrees with firmware).
findings.why_matters: When live FLIGHTMODE telemetry arrives with M1, the HUD displays 'RACE' while the firmware/ChannelDecoder consider mode 1 to be 'Gearbox' (plain gearbox, ERS OFF — main.cpp:334 only enables ERS when driveMode==2). The owner reading 'RACE' has no idea ERS is disabled in that mode. Mode 0 (Training) is the only unambiguous one.
findings.expected: HUD labels match the firmware's semantic names, or the firmware and docs agree on one naming.
findings.suspected: Same numbers, contradictory names across firmware, TELEMETRY.md, and HUD.
findings.how_to_verify: Feed FLIGHTMODE 'G2 M1 E80'; HUD shows 'RACE'; firmware treats mode 1 as ERS-off Gearbox.
findings.fix_direction: Pick one naming across ChannelDecoder.hpp, link2_protocol.md, TELEMETRY.md, and hud.js DRIVE_MODES.

findings.title: HUD simulated gear count (8) contradicts firmware gearbox (4), skewing sim gear/rev-light behavior
findings.evidence: feelConstants.js:12 `GEARS: 8`. hud.js:35 FEEL.gears=8, used in shift() clamp (hud.js:90), computeCaps (hud.js:39), rev-light 'shifting' (hud.js:171,182), demo shift logic. Firmware lib/gearbox/include/gearbox/Gearbox.hpp:26 `numGears = 4`; main.cpp:381 displayGear=currentGear()+1 (so 1..4). link2_protocol.md says gear 1..6.
findings.why_matters: With no telemetry the HUD lets the owner shift up to gear 8 and computes rev/shift behavior on an 8-speed curve the car does not have; the gamepad 'mirror' therefore does NOT mirror the car's actual gearing. When live gear arrives (1..4) it's shown directly, so the widget silently switches gear-space depending on source. Three different gear counts exist across the system (4 / 6 / 8).
findings.expected: One authoritative gear count shared with the firmware (ideally sourced like the ERS constants, which feelConstants.js already syncs).
findings.suspected: HUD sim uses 8 gears; car has 4.
findings.how_to_verify: In demo/no-telemetry, upshift repeatedly — reaches gear 8; car only has 4.
findings.fix_direction: Set GEARS to the firmware numGears (4) and reconcile link2_protocol.md; add a test guarding it against drift as done for ERS rates.

findings.title: mediamtx camera source and H.265/H.264 gate are unverified placeholders (video path unproven)
findings.evidence: mediamtx.yml:29 `source: rtsp://192.168.1.10:554/stream0` is a placeholder IP/path (SETUP.md:24 says get the real URL from majestic_fpv.yaml). SETUP.md:7-17 flags that Chromium/Electron WebRTC generally cannot decode H.265/HEVC and the SSC338Q often defaults to H.265 — whep.js negotiates video recvonly with no codec constraint, so an H.265 stream will connect but never render frames.
findings.why_matters: The entire video half of the ground station is gated on a camera fact not yet verified. If the camera is H.265 and can't be reconfigured, the WHEP path shows a permanently 'WAITING FOR VIDEO' underlay while everything else looks fine. Correctly documented as highest-risk, but unverifiable pre-hardware.
findings.expected: Real RTSP URL set and camera confirmed emitting H.264 (or transcode configured).
findings.suspected: Placeholder source; codec unknown until bench.
findings.how_to_verify: Bench: point source at real camera, check majestic.yaml video0.codec, confirm WHEP renders in the Electron window.
findings.fix_direction: Set real source; force camera to H.264 or add an ffmpeg transcode stage; VLC-on-RTSP is the documented fallback.

findings.title: README claims failsafe (and per-field) telemetry the app never delivers
findings.evidence: README.md:12 'Telemetry overlay — real speed, gear, drive mode, ERS%, battery, link quality and failsafe'. No source populates `failsafe` (see frameToTelemetry in shared/crsfTelemetry.js; firmware sends no failsafe frame). TELEMETRY.md:12-13 also lists armed/failsafe in the contract table as if deliverable.
findings.why_matters: Docs overstate what the real path provides; combined with the LINK LOST finding, it creates false confidence that link-loss is surfaced.
findings.expected: Docs distinguish fields the real (crsf-serial) source can deliver from fields only the replay/demo source scripts.
findings.suspected: armed/failsafe are contract-listed and README-advertised but only ever set by the demo timeline.
findings.how_to_verify: grep for `failsafe:` assignments in shared/ — only replaySource.js DEMO_TIMELINE sets it.
findings.fix_direction: Mark armed/failsafe as demo-only / future, or implement them end-to-end.

findings.title: Stale merged telemetry fields are never cleared, so old values persist after a frame type stops
findings.evidence: CrsfSerialSource._telem is Object.assign-accumulated (lines 33,88) and never pruned. If, e.g., the car stops sending GPS but battery keeps arriving, the last speedKmh persists indefinitely and is re-emitted on every battery frame. The renderer's 1s staleness fallback (hud.js:158) only triggers when the ENTIRE source stops emitting, not per-field.
findings.why_matters: A frozen-but-nonzero speed or gear could display as live during a partial telemetry fault (one frame type wedged). Low severity because full-source loss is caught, but per-field staleness is not.
findings.expected: Per-field age tracking, or accept and document that fields are last-known-value until the whole source goes stale.
findings.suspected: Individual fields go stale silently while the source is still emitting other frames.
findings.how_to_verify: Feed battery frames continuously and one GPS frame, then stop GPS; observe speedKmh stays at the last GPS value.
findings.fix_direction: Track per-field timestamps or expire fields not refreshed within N seconds.

findings.title: Non-Windows telemetry-port default and CRSF baud for the reader are unproven assumptions
findings.evidence: main.js:38 defaults W17_TELEMETRY_PORT to 'COM5' (win) / '/dev/ttyUSB0' (else). CrsfSerialSource constructor defaults baud=420000 (CrsfSerialSource.js:19). The com0com/hub4com virtual-port workaround (TELEMETRY.md) mirrors bytes regardless of baud, but if the reader end is a real FT232 the 420000 must match what elrs-joystick-control set; over a virtual pipe the baud is nominal. The port default is a guess (docs say 'COMx').
findings.why_matters: Minor: wrong defaults just fail to open and the app degrades gracefully with a retry log. But there is no confirmed value and the whole path is bench-gated (COM-port exclusivity).
findings.expected: Confirmed port + baud once the com0com/forward route is chosen on the bench.
findings.suspected: Defaults are placeholders; unverifiable without hardware.
findings.how_to_verify: Bench: set the real reader COM port; confirm CrsfSerialSource logs 'CRSF serial open' and battery/LQ populate.
findings.fix_direction: Document the confirmed port/baud after bench bring-up; keep the graceful-degrade log.

findings.title: No raw-frame / decode-error diagnostics for bring-up; failures are near-silent
findings.evidence: CrsfAssembler.feedByte silently drops CRC-bad or malformed frames (returns null, crsfAssembler.js:41); CrsfSerialSource never counts or logs decode failures — only open/error/close are logged. There is no HUD or console readout of frames/sec, CRC-error rate, or LINK_STATISTICS RSSI/SNR (decodeLinkStatistics decodes them but frameToTelemetry discards everything except uplinkLinkQuality, crsfTelemetry.js:31).
findings.why_matters: During real bring-up (wrong baud, byte framing off a virtual splitter, half-duplex garbage) the operator gets no signal beyond 'battery/LQ didn't light up.' The tool 'looks pretty' but offers little to diagnose why telemetry isn't arriving.
findings.expected: A debug affordance: frame/error counters, last-frame-type, and the decoded RSSI/SNR fields surfaced somewhere.
findings.suspected: Silent frame drops make telemetry bring-up hard to debug.
findings.how_to_verify: Point the source at a mis-baud'd stream: nothing logs; HUD just stays 'sim'.
findings.fix_direction: Add optional verbose logging of frame types + CRC-fail counts in CrsfSerialSource; optionally surface RSSI/SNR on the HUD.

open_questions: Does elrs-joystick-control actually expose a telemetry forward (UDP/stdout), or must the com0com/hub4com splitter be used? The entire real-telemetry path hinges on this and is unverified (TELEMETRY.md lists it as the first thing to check).

Will the firmware ever transmit an armed/failsafe indication over the ELRS backchannel, or should the ground derive failsafe from LINK_STATISTICS uplinkLinkQuality==0? The HUD's LINK LOST logic assumes a failsafe field that does not exist on the real path.
Is the OpenIPC camera H.264-capable, or will an ffmpeg transcode be required? This determines whether the Electron video path works at all vs. the VLC fallback.
Which gear count is authoritative (firmware 4 / link2 doc 6 / HUD 8)? The mirror only mirrors the car if these agree.

hw_validation_items.item: Confirm the real camera RTSP URL from majestic_fpv.yaml and set mediamtx.yml paths.cam.source; confirm the WHEP endpoint renders video in the Electron window (not just connects).
Check the OpenIPC SSC338Q video0.codec; if H.265, reconfigure to H.264 or add an ffmpeg transcode into mediamtx — WebRTC in Electron will not decode H.265.

Establish the CRSF telemetry reader path (elrs-joystick-control forward vs com0com/hub4com), set W17_TELEMETRY_PORT, and confirm CrsfSerialSource logs 'CRSF serial open' and battery/speed/gear/LQ go live on the HUD.

Verify the TX module actually emits LINK_STATISTICS (0x14) and the car's BATTERY (0x08)/GPS (0x02)/FLIGHTMODE (0x21) on that serial at a usable rate; confirm decoded values match the car's real battery/speed/gear/mode/ERS.
Deliberately drop the ELRS link (power the RX down) and confirm what the HUD shows — currently it will NOT show LINK LOST from the real path; validate the desired behavior before relying on it.
On Windows, run npx electron-rebuild for serialport against Electron 31 ABI and confirm the packaged (asarUnpack'd) build loads the .node at runtime.

dimensions.dimension: All communication protocols end to end (CRSF RX->control, link2 control->soundlight, CRSF telemetry backchannel control->ELRS->ground)
dimensions.summary: The three protocols are unusually disciplined for a hobby build: CRSF and link2 both use a stateful byte-stream assembler that resyncs on the next start/sync byte after any failure, hard-rejects an unsupported length byte the moment it arrives (preventing a corrupt 0xFF length from swallowing ~1s of stream), and validates CRC before trusting anything. Byte order is internally consistent and matches on both encode/decode sides on every path I checked: CRSF channels little-endian bit-packed (packChannels/unpackChannels are exact inverses), CRSF telemetry payloads big-endian (battery voltage/capacity, GPS groundspeed, altitude) verified against ground-station decoders with explicit byte-offset tests, and link2 multi-byte fields little-endian on both control encode and soundlight decode. The CRC is a correct CRC-8/DVB-S2 (poly 0xD5, init 0), pinned by the 0xBC "123456789" catalog KAT in all three repos, and link2's deliberately-duplicated CRC is cross-checked against CRSF's in a test. The two sim feeders (SimCrsfFeeder, SimLink2Feeder) both build frames through the real buildRcChannelsFrame/encodeFrame and feed them through the real feedByte path, so the sim exercises production parsing, not a shortcut. Real defects are at the semantic/agreement layer rather than the wire layer: a driveMode label divergence between firmware and HUD, an unenforced/inconsistent gear range across the system, a misleading claim that firmware golden vectors are shared with the ground station (they are not — each repo self-tests with its own frame builder), and the entire telemetry uplink routing/addressing is unverified against real ELRS hardware. No acks/retransmit exist on either link, which is correct for these one-way real-time streams because both receivers implement a staleness timeout.
well_designed: CRSF assembler (CrsfFrameAssembler.cpp) is type-agnostic and resyncs correctly: FrameReady means 'CRC-valid frame of ANY type', so interleaved LINK_STATISTICS/telemetry is not treated as corruption, and per-type payload-length validation is pushed into CrsfReceiver.cpp (a CRC-valid RC frame with wrong payload length is ignored, not decoded — test_receiver_ignores_known_type_with_wrong_payload_length).

Both assemblers hard-reject an unsupported length byte the instant it arrives (CRSF: expectedLength_<2 || >kMaxFrameLen=64 -> reset+FrameInvalid; link2 ReadingLength: b!=kPayloadLen -> immediate FrameInvalid), so a corrupt length can't swallow up to 255 following bytes before resync. Explicitly tested (test_assembler_resyncs_after_corrupted_frame, link2 equivalents).

CRC is a correct CRC-8/DVB-S2 (poly 0xD5, MSB-first, init 0), pinned by the catalog check value 0xBC for '123456789' as an independent KAT in firmware (test_crc8_known_answer_test_vector), link2, and ground-station (crsf.test.js). The three implementations are byte-for-byte the same loop.

link2 validation order is start -> length -> CRC -> version (Link2Codec.cpp decodeFrame), so a corrupted version byte reports CrcMismatch and BadVersion means exactly 'well-formed frame from a newer protocol' — clean forward-compat semantics. Flag bit7 is reserved and receivers mask rather than reject.

link2 duplicates the CRSF CRC8 on purpose (so lib/link2 lifts wholesale into board #2 with no lib/crsf dependency) AND a test cross-checks the two implementations against each other (test_link2/test_main.cpp lines 74-75: crsf::computeCrc8 == link2::computeCrc8 over the same data).

Both sim feeders exercise the REAL parser path, not a shortcut: SimCrsfFeeder.cpp calls crsf::buildRcChannelsFrame then main.cpp feeds those bytes through crsfReceiver.feedByte; SimLink2Feeder.cpp calls link2::encodeFrame then soundlight main.cpp feeds bytes through monitor.feedByte. This is exactly the property you want from a sim.

CRSF telemetry payload byte order is verified on both sides with concrete offset tests: firmware test_build_battery_frame_bytes / test_build_gps_frame_groundspeed_be assert big-endian hi/lo byte positions and 24-bit capacity, and ground-station decodeBattery/decodeGps read the same offsets big-endian. GPS groundspeed 361 = 0x0169 at payload[8..9] agrees end to end (crsfTelemetry.test.js line 47).

link2 receiver treats 'no CRC-valid frame for 500 ms' as link loss (Link2Monitor stalenessMs=500 = 10 missed frames at 20 Hz nominal) and holds last-known gear/ers/driveMode while forcing safe dynamic fields — the correct design for a one-way link where a cut wire is otherwise indistinguishable from 'last state persists'. Documented in Link2Frame.hpp header.

CrsfReceiver correctly keeps an OWNED copy of channels (channels_.channels) rather than pointing into the shared assembler buffer, and LINK_STATISTICS frames do NOT bump lastRcFrameMs (test_receiver_stats_do_not_bump_rc_frame_time) so a LQ=0 loss burst can't extend the failsafe timeout — both are real, tested regression guards.

findings.title: crsf.js comment claims firmware golden vectors are reused, but the ground station has NO shared golden fixtures — each repo self-tests with its own frame builder
findings.evidence: shared/crsf.js lines 1-5: 'ported FAITHFULLY from the firmware ... The firmware's golden vectors are reused as tests.' But test/crsf.test.js (line 15) and test/crsfTelemetry.test.js (line 14) each define their OWN local buildFrame() that calls the JS computeCrc8 to fill the CRC. No byte array produced by the C++ CrsfFrameBuilder is imported or hard-coded. The battery-frame comment (crsfTelemetry.test.js line 22) says it 'matches the control repo's test_build_battery_frame_bytes golden vector' but the bytes are regenerated by the JS builder, not copied.
findings.why_matters: The JS decode and C++ encode are the one piece of genuine cross-repo shared truth, and they are pinned only by prose + a shared CRC algorithm, not by a shared byte fixture. If someone changes a byte offset, endianness, or the CRC span on ONE side, that repo's tests keep passing (they use that side's own builder) and the other repo's tests keep passing too — the disagreement is invisible until real hardware. This is precisely the silent-divergence risk the comment claims is covered.
findings.expected: A committed golden fixture (e.g. the exact bytes 0xC8,0x0A,0x08,0x00,0x4F,... for the 7.9V/72% battery frame) shared or duplicated verbatim, asserted byte-for-byte in both the firmware test and the JS test, so a change on either side breaks the other's build.
findings.suspected: Byte-identical layout is asserted independently on each side; a regression in one repo's wire format would not be caught by the other's tests despite the comment implying it would.
findings.how_to_verify: grep both test dirs for a hard-coded frame byte array shared across repos — there is none. Confirm crsf.test.js buildFrame and crsfTelemetry.test.js buildFrame both call the local computeCrc8 rather than embedding fixed CRC bytes.
findings.fix_direction: Add a small shared golden-vectors file (or duplicate the exact bytes with a comment 'generated by firmware CrsfFrameBuilder, do not edit') for battery/GPS/flightmode/RC frames and assert them byte-for-byte in BOTH repos. Optionally add a CI step that diffs the vectors.

findings.title: driveMode field: firmware/link2 protocol labels ('1=Gearbox, 2=Gearbox+ERS') disagree with the ground-station HUD labels ('1=RACE, 2=ERS')
findings.evidence: w17-control-fw/lib/link2/include/link2/Link2Frame.hpp line 65 and docs/link2_protocol.md line 43: '0 = Training, 1 = Gearbox, 2 = Gearbox+ERS'. w17-ground-station/renderer/hud.js lines 22-28 DRIVE_MODES = [{TRAINING},{RACE},{ERS}] indexed by telem.driveMode, with the comment '0=Training, 1=Race, 2=ERS'. Same numeric index, different displayed name.
findings.why_matters: The number transmitted over FLIGHTMODE 'M<n>' and over link2 byte[12] is consistent (0/1/2), so nothing breaks functionally, but the HUD shows the driver 'RACE'/'ERS' while the firmware and protocol doc call the same modes 'Gearbox'/'Gearbox+ERS'. On a gift car this is a user-facing inconsistency and a maintenance trap: a future reader trusting one source mislabels the mode.
findings.expected: One canonical set of drive-mode names used by firmware, link2 doc, TELEMETRY.md, and the HUD.
findings.suspected: Two divergent naming schemes over identical numbers, reconciled nowhere.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare Link2Frame.hpp line 65 / link2_protocol.md line 43 against hud.js DRIVE_MODES and docs/TELEMETRY.md.
findings.fix_direction: Pick one naming (the HUD's RACE/ERS is the driver-facing choice) and update the other three sources to match, or add a note that Gearbox==RACE.

findings.title: Gear range is inconsistent across the system (link2 doc 1..6, control-fw gearbox numGears=4, HUD FEEL.gears=8) and the FLIGHTMODE 'G<n>' field is unbounded
findings.evidence: w17-control-fw/docs/link2_protocol.md line 39: 'gear ... 1-based display gear, 1…6.' w17-control-fw/lib/gearbox/include/gearbox/Gearbox.hpp line 26: 'uint8_t numGears = 4;'. w17-ground-station/renderer/hud.js line 35: 'FEEL = { gears: 8, ...}'. main.cpp line 287 formats the FLIGHTMODE string with '%u' on controlSnapshot.displayGear with no range clamp.
findings.why_matters: The car actually has 4 gears; the protocol doc promises up to 6; the HUD's simulation assumes 8. When live FLIGHTMODE telemetry arrives, hud.js line 169 shows telem.gear verbatim, so a real gear (1..4) will display fine, but the HUD's own simulated gear caps table (computeCaps over 8 gears) and its shift logic are calibrated for 8 gears — the sim and the live display use different gear universes. Nothing crashes, but speed/gear feel in demo mode does not match the car, and the doc misdescribes the hardware.
findings.expected: A single documented gear count (4) propagated to the gearbox default, the link2 doc, and the HUD FEEL constant, or an explicit statement that the HUD sim is intentionally a different scale.
findings.suspected: Three different gear counts coexist with no reconciliation; FLIGHTMODE 'G<n>' can in principle carry a value the HUD's caps table never generated.
findings.how_to_verify: Compare the three cited lines; confirm main.cpp does not clamp displayGear before snprintf.
findings.fix_direction: Set one canonical gear count (match the gearbox's 4), update link2_protocol.md and hud.js FEEL.gears, and clamp/validate gear in the FLIGHTMODE formatter if desired.

findings.title: CRSF telemetry frames are built with sync/address byte 0xC8 and pushed straight out UART2 TX — whether RP1/ELRS actually relays arbitrary battery/GPS/FLIGHTMODE sensor frames sent this way is unverified against hardware
findings.evidence: buildFrame (CrsfFrameBuilder.hpp line 35) always writes outFrame[0]=kSyncByte=0xC8 (CrsfFrame.hpp line 20). main.cpp lines 256-294 write battery(0x08), GPS(0x02), and FLIGHTMODE(0x21) frames directly to crsfUart every 200ms. CLAUDE.md marks the whole telemetry return path BENCH-GATED; there has been no real-hardware validation.
findings.why_matters: Standard CRSF device->handset telemetry over ELRS depends on the receiver accepting and forwarding the sensor frame; ELRS receivers are selective about which telemetry frame types they relay and about the addressing byte on the device->RX link. If RP1 does not forward 0x02/0x21 (or expects a different first byte for locally-originated telemetry), the ground station will simply never see speed/gear/mode even though the firmware and JS decoders agree perfectly. This blocks the telemetry HUD feature at bring-up, not the car's control.
findings.expected: Confirmation on the bench that RP1 relays 0x08, 0x02 and 0x21 frames emitted with a 0xC8 first byte at 420000 baud, and that the ground TX passes them through.
findings.suspected: Unknown whether ELRS forwards these frame types / this addressing; possible that only some (e.g. battery 0x08) are relayed and GPS/FLIGHTMODE are dropped.
findings.how_to_verify: On the bench: emit each frame type from the control board, watch the ground station CrsfSerialSource for decoded battery/GPS/flightmode; if some types are missing, check ELRS telemetry-forwarding docs and whether an extended-frame (dest/origin) header is required.
findings.fix_direction: Verify per frame type on hardware; if ELRS requires extended addressing or a specific origin byte for relayed sensors, adjust buildFrame for those types.

findings.title: No CI/check keeps the duplicated lib/link2 (Link2Codec/Frame + link2_protocol.md) in sync between control-fw and soundlight-fw
findings.evidence: Established fact (verified by lead auditor and consistent with what I read): lib/link2 is BYTE-IDENTICAL between the two repos but COPIED, not shared, and no CI diff enforces it. The soundlight monitor decodes with soundlight's copy of decodeFrame; the control board encodes with control's copy.
findings.why_matters: link2 is the control->sound/light contract. If someone edits the payload layout, a flag bit, or the CRC span in ONE repo (e.g. adds a v2 field), the other repo's copy silently diverges and the two boards disagree on the wire with no test catching it — exactly the failure the cross-repo golden-vector gap (finding above) also leaves open. The in-repo encode/decode round-trip tests pass in each repo independently regardless of divergence.
findings.expected: Either a shared submodule/package for lib/link2, or a CI job that diffs the two copies (and link2_protocol.md) and fails on mismatch.
findings.suspected: Manual copy discipline is the only thing keeping the two boards' link2 in agreement.
findings.how_to_verify: diff the two lib/link2 trees (currently identical) and grep CI configs for any cross-repo diff check (none exists).
findings.fix_direction: Add a CI check that fails if the two lib/link2 dirs + link2_protocol.md differ, or extract link2 into a shared dependency.

findings.title: Battery millivolts are integer-divided by 100 to decivolts, discarding the low digit before transmission (max ~0.09 V loss)
findings.evidence: main.cpp line 265: 'const uint16_t deciVolt = static_cast<uint16_t>(mv / 100); // mV -> 0.1V'. e.g. 7990 mV -> 79 -> HUD shows 7.9 V (0.09 V low). No rounding.
findings.why_matters: Purely a display precision issue on a monitoring-only value (CLAUDE.md 6.4 says battery telemetry is monitoring, not auto-cut), so it cannot cause unsafe behavior. Worth noting only because it is a silent truncation, not a round.
findings.expected: Round to nearest decivolt: (mv + 50) / 100.
findings.suspected: Truncation toward zero, up to one decivolt low.
findings.how_to_verify: Read main.cpp line 265; compare against decodeBattery dividing by 10 in crsf.js line 70.
findings.fix_direction: Use (mv + 50)/100 if the extra 0.05 V matters; otherwise document the truncation.

open_questions: Does RP1/ELRS actually relay battery(0x08), GPS(0x02) and FLIGHTMODE(0x21) telemetry frames emitted with a 0xC8 first byte at 420000 baud, or is an extended (dest/origin) frame header required for locally-originated sensor telemetry? Cannot be answered without the FT232+ELRS TX+RP1 bench chain.

The FLIGHTMODE 'M<n>' number is transmitted, but is there any consumer that maps M0/M1/M2 to the divergent Gearbox/RACE labels, or does only the HUD's DRIVE_MODES array interpret it? If a handset also displays the raw string, the label divergence surfaces there too.

link2 flag bit7 is reserved and receivers mask it, but is there a planned v2 field that will need it? If v2 adds a field, the version-before-CRC ordering means old soundlight firmware will report BadVersion and hold last-known state — is that the intended degrade behavior for a mixed-firmware bring-up?

The HUD prefers live telem.gear/driveMode/ersPct over its local simulation, but the FLIGHTMODE regex parseFlightMode is order/spacing tolerant and returns only matched fields — if the car ever emits a partial string (e.g. truncated by kFlightModeMaxLen), does the HUD gracefully fall back per-field? The truncation test only covers the pure ASCII path, not a mid-token cut.

hw_validation_items.item: On the bench, emit each CRSF telemetry frame type (0x08 battery, 0x02 GPS/groundspeed, 0x21 flightmode) from the control board and confirm the ground station CrsfSerialSource decodes each one — some ELRS builds forward only a subset of sensor frame types.

Confirm the control->soundlight link2 UART actually agrees on the wire: capture control TX (GPIO25) at 115200 8N1 and confirm soundlight's Link2Monitor reports FrameReady (not FrameInvalid/BadVersion) for real frames — verifies the two COPIED lib/link2 trees are still in sync in flashed firmware.

Verify CRSF RX->control at 420000 baud NOT inverted: confirm CrsfReceiver produces NewRcFrame and linkUp() true from the real RP1, and that a deliberate antenna-off event drives uplinkLinkQuality to 0 and latches rxSignalsFailsafe.
Scope/log the telemetry send cadence (200ms) under load to confirm the three telemetry writes plus link2 do not stall the 50Hz control tick or overflow the CRSF TX FIFO at 420000 baud.

Confirm the live FLIGHTMODE gear value shown on the HUD matches the car's actual gear across a full up/down shift sweep (guards against the 4/6/8 gear-count divergence producing a surprising display).

dimensions.dimension: Simulation and automated-test coverage — what is actually PROVEN
dimensions.summary: The pure-logic test suites are genuinely strong: 147+40+21 tests exercise the REAL production code (tests #include the same headers main.cpp does; there is no duplicated/simplified logic path), use dependency-injected mocks that assert commanded microseconds/bytes rather than tautologies, and pin exact byte vectors, boundary transitions, and named regression findings. The control-fw Wokwi sim is the best part of the whole system: esp32dev_sim runs the byte-for-byte identical setup()/loop() as the shipped firmware and feeds CRSF through a real Serial2 TX->RX loopback, so the actual UART driver + parser + failsafe + arm gate run unmodified. BUT the green suite proves almost nothing about hardware: every *_hal_esp32 file (Hall ISR, LEDC PWM, ADC, NVS, I2S) is entirely untested and unsimulatable, the ISR's atomic/torn-read concurrency and 2ms lockout are unverified, and the entire CRSF telemetry return path is bench-gated. Critically, the soundlight-fw has NO wokwi.toml/diagram.json at all — its "Stage 2 sim" only compiles in CI and its SimLink2Feeder bypasses the real UART by injecting straight into the monitor, so board #2's actual I2S audio, WS2812 timing, dual-core audio race, and real serial RX have zero pre-hardware validation. The cross-repo link2 protocol and ERS/feel constants are pinned by duplicated golden vectors with no CI that would catch the two copies drifting apart. Confidence: high that the decode/state-machine/scaling MATH is correct; low that the assembled firmware behaves correctly on real silicon.
well_designed: Tests exercise the real production logic, not a reimplementation: e.g. control test/test_crsf builds frames with the SAME crsf/CrsfFrameBuilder.hpp the sim uses and decodes with the production crsf::decodeFrame/CrsfReceiver; link2 tests include the real link2/Link2Codec.hpp. No 'shadow logic' that could pass while firmware fails.

esp32dev_sim is the real firmware, verified: control-fw/platformio.ini [env:esp32dev_sim] merely `extends = env:esp32dev` plus `-DW17_SIM_CRSF_FEEDER`; main.cpp guards only the feeder call (line 204-208) and status prints behind that flag, so setup()/loop()/the 50Hz control tick are byte-identical to the shipped esp32dev build. The sim feeds Serial2 which diagram.json loops back to RX2 (`[ "esp:TX2", "esp:RX2" ]`), so the genuine UART RX + CrsfReceiver run.

Failsafe transitions are tested exhaustively as a pure state machine (test_failsafe): boots Safe, never-goes-Active-with-no-frame (regression A1), timeout boundary inclusive, rx-failsafe-flag wins over fresh frames, latch-despite-single-good-tick, and re-arm-window chatter reset. This is the safety-critical module and it has the deepest coverage.

The hold-position/misconfigured-RX hazard is directly tested AND demonstrated in the sim: test_receiver_failsafe_flag_survives_rc_frames_without_fresh_stats feeds ~5s of bogus RC frames after an LQ=0 burst and asserts the latch holds; the Wokwi HOLD_POSITION_FAILSAFE phase (19-21s) reproduces it end-to-end at 50% throttle.

ArmGate 'no arm-into-full-throttle' is pinned from multiple angles (test_channels): blocks arm with displaced stick, requires fresh neutral after switch-off AND after a failsafe episode (regression A3), force-disarm polarity, and the +/-60 neutral-window boundary. The ESC boot-arm hold is anchored to first-command (regression A5) and boundary-tested inclusive.

Frame assemblers are hardened against real serial garbage: both CRSF and link2 assemblers test byte-by-byte framing, garbage-without-sync rejection, resync-after-corrupt-CRC, hard-reject of a 0xFF length byte (avoids swallowing 255 bytes), and — cleverly — link2 resync when the 0xA5 start byte legally appears inside a payload (throttlePercent=-91).

Numeric edge cases are covered where they matter: channel normalization clamps the full 11-bit 0..2047 range (not just 172..1811), truncates toward neutral, gearbox output is asserted monotonic-nondecreasing across a sweep, ERS uses an exact micro-permille accumulator with a dt-stall clamp test, and WheelSpeed handles first-edge-no-period, implausible-period clamp (EMI), and graceful decay/timeout.

Cross-implementation CRC agreement is pinned three ways (crsf vs link2 vs settings computeCrc8) plus the CRC-8/DVB-S2 catalog check value 0xBC, and the ground-station JS computeCrc8 independently reproduces 0xBC — so the four separate CRC copies are proven mutually consistent by test.

soundlight test_integration is a genuine hardware-free end-to-end chain: encoded link2 bytes -> Link2Monitor -> EngineSim -> EngineSynth.render (real audio buffers, peak-checked for clipping) -> LightRenderer, including a link-loss phase that asserts engine->Off/silence and hazard-amber lights. This is the cheapest real confidence for board #2's logic.

Ground-station replay/timeline tests pin the enum-vs-interpolate distinction (driveMode steps, never 1.5), boolean stepping during link loss, and a feelConstants test that guards ERS_DEPLOY/HARVEST/BOOST against drift from the firmware ErsConfig — an explicit cross-repo drift guard.

findings.title: soundlight-fw has no Wokwi sim at all — board #2 has zero pre-hardware end-to-end validation
findings.evidence: The repo has no wokwi.toml or diagram.json (ls of *.json/*.toml returns none; only platformio.ini). CI builds esp32dev_sim but nothing simulates it. src/main.cpp lines 107-117 inject sim frames via `monitor.feedByte(...)` directly, bypassing Serial2 entirely — the real UART RX path (Serial2.begin at line 90, drain loop lines 103-105) is never exercised even by the feeder.
findings.why_matters: CLAUDE.md/README claim a 'Stage 2' sim, but for board #2 it is flash-to-real-hardware-only. The dual-core audio task race (gSynthParams/gControlHeartbeatMs atomics, xTaskCreatePinnedToCore core 0 vs Arduino core 1), the I2S DMA + WS2812 show() timing interaction, the deadman mute, and real 115200 8N1 framing/overrun get NO validation until parts arrive. Unlike control-fw, there is no loopback proving the actual serial parser works on-chip.
findings.expected: A wokwi.toml + diagram.json for board #2 (ESP32 + I2S/NeoPixel parts, and ideally a second-UART feeder) mirroring control-fw, OR the SimLink2Feeder routed through a real Serial loopback so the on-chip UART path is exercised.
findings.suspected: esp32dev_sim only compiles in CI; it is never run as a simulation, and the feeder never touches the UART driver.
findings.how_to_verify: Confirm absence of wokwi.toml/diagram.json in w17-soundlight-fw; read main.cpp lines 107-117 (feeds monitor, not Serial2).
findings.fix_direction: Add a Wokwi diagram for board #2, or at minimum route SimLink2Feeder output through Serial (TX->RX loopback) like control-fw does, so the real UART RX + monitor path runs in sim.

findings.title: All *_hal_esp32 hardware code is untested and unsimulated (ISR concurrency, LEDC, ADC, NVS, I2S)
findings.evidence: grep for 'hal_esp32' across both test/ trees returns nothing — no test references any HAL. platformio.ini native env lib_ignores them. The Hall ISR (onEdge, IRAM_ATTR) holds count_ and lastPeriodUs_ as separate std::atomic<uint32_t> and read() loads them independently (lines: count_.load / lastPeriodUs_.load in read()), so a snapshot can pair a fresh count with a stale period (torn across two edges). edgeSeen_/lastEdgeUs_ are non-atomic ISR-only state. The 2ms kLockoutUs debounce and RISING-edge attach are never tested.
findings.why_matters: WheelSpeed pure logic is well tested but assumes the snapshot {count, periodUs} is coherent; a torn read feeds a wrong period into the rpm math (and thus ERS harvest gating, GPS telemetry, and sound rpm). None of this — nor ADC attenuation, LEDC channel setup, NVS blob persistence, or I2S channel_format — can be caught by any native test or by Wokwi (Wokwi models the pins but not EMI, ADC nonlinearity, DMA underruns, or the real IDF driver semantics the soundlight platform pin depends on).
findings.expected: A documented hardware-in-the-loop checklist (some exists in SIMULATION.md) PLUS at least a reasoning/review that the ISR snapshot is coherent, or a seqlock/single-atomic-struct so count+period cannot tear.
findings.suspected: Torn count/period snapshot possible; all HAL behavior unproven until bench.
findings.how_to_verify: Read Esp32HallPulseCounter::read() — two independent atomic loads with no seqlock. On the bench: scope the Hall line under ESC EMI and confirm rpm does not spike.
findings.fix_direction: Consider a seqlock or packing count+period into one atomic<uint64_t>; add the ISR/ADC/LEDC/I2S items to a first-power validation checklist and verify on the bench.

findings.title: Cross-repo link2 protocol and ERS constants are pinned by DUPLICATED golden vectors with no CI drift guard
findings.evidence: control test/test_link2 and soundlight test/test_link2 each hard-code the SAME kGoldenFrame {0xA5,0x0B,...,0xCE} in their own source (soundlight comment: 'copied VERBATIM from w17-control-fw'). The lead-auditor baseline confirms lib/link2 is byte-identical between repos with NO CI check keeping them in sync. Ground-station feelConstants.test.js pins ERS_DEPLOY=26/HARVEST=11/BOOST=1.18 against ErsConfig but lives in a third repo with a separate CI.
findings.why_matters: Each repo's tests will stay green after a one-sided edit to its own copy of the golden frame or the codec, because nothing compares the two copies. A protocol change on the sender that is mirrored wrongly (or not at all) on the receiver would pass both suites independently and only surface on the bench as garbled sound/lights.
findings.expected: A CI step (or shared submodule/package) that diffs the two lib/link2 copies and the two golden vectors, failing on divergence.
findings.suspected: Three independent copies of the protocol truth, each self-consistent, no automated cross-check.
findings.how_to_verify: Confirm the two kGoldenFrame arrays are identical today; confirm no workflow diffs them (the two ci.yml files only run their own repo's tests).
findings.fix_direction: Add a CI diff of lib/link2 + docs/link2_protocol.md across repos, or extract link2 into a shared vendored package with one source of truth.

findings.title: main.cpp control-loop wiring/orchestration has no test — only the sim covers it, and the sim is not asserted in CI
findings.evidence: platformio.ini native env sets test_build_src = no, so main.cpp (the loop that sequences failsafe -> armGate -> gearbox -> ERS applyBoost -> outputs, plus the rcFrameSinceTick accumulation at lines 214-220 and the phase-accumulating tick guard at line 303) is never compiled into any test. The only thing exercising it is the Wokwi sim, and SIMULATION.md's own 'Future hook (not built)' section admits wokwi-cli serial assertions are NOT wired into CI — the sim is a manual, eyeball-only check.
findings.why_matters: The individual modules are proven, but their COMPOSITION (order of operations, the boost-after-arm-gate invariant, the freeze-ERS-in-failsafe dt handling, gear-edge consume-once, the Safe-branch-still-transmits-link2 behavior) is only informally validated. A regression in main.cpp's sequencing would pass all 147 native tests and only be caught if a human watches the Wokwi serial narration.
findings.expected: Either a host-compilable integration test of the orchestration (like soundlight's test_integration, which DOES exist for board #2), or wokwi-cli scenario assertions run in CI.
findings.suspected: Orchestration correctness rests on manual sim observation, not automated assertion.
findings.how_to_verify: Read platformio.ini (test_build_src = no) and SIMULATION.md 'Future hook (not built)'. Note soundlight has test_integration but control-fw has no equivalent main-path test.
findings.fix_direction: Add a control-fw host integration test that drives decode->failsafe->armgate->gearbox->ers->snapshot with canned Controls, mirroring soundlight test_integration; and/or wire wokwi-cli serial-assertion scenarios into CI.

findings.title: Several coverage gaps in named scenarios: NVS-corruption on real flash, ADC saturation extremes, Hall glitch/bounce, staleness-during-boot for board #2
findings.evidence: Settings tests cover corrupt-blob/wrong-version/truncated/out-of-range at the CODEC level, but Esp32NvsStore.cpp (real NVS read/write/erase, partition-full, wear) is untested. BatteryMonitor tests inject pinMillivolts but never the ADC rails (0 mV, >2450 mV clip at 11dB, or a floating/disconnected divider); Esp32BatteryAdc's 4-read averaging is untested. WheelSpeed clamps an implausible period in pure logic, but real contact-bounce/EMI bursts hitting the ISR lockout are only asserted in the SIMULATION.md checklist, not tested. soundlight Link2Monitor boots NeverConnected+failsafe (good) but there is no test of a partial/interrupted first frame at boot arriving mid-byte.
findings.why_matters: These are exactly the messy-real-world inputs the audit brief flags. The green suite proves the math handles clean synthetic extremes; it does not prove the firmware survives a corrupted NVS partition, a shorted/open battery divider, or ISR bounce under motor EMI.
findings.expected: Explicit bench tests for NVS corruption (write garbage to the partition, confirm defaults load), ADC open/short reads, and Hall bounce under EMI; a boot-time partial-frame test for board #2 would be pure-logic and cheap.
findings.suspected: Real-flash NVS, ADC rail behavior, and ISR bounce are unvalidated; a cheap boot partial-frame test is missing.
findings.how_to_verify: Read Esp32NvsStore.cpp / Esp32BatteryAdc.cpp (no test references). Confirm no test feeds a mid-frame boot to Link2Monitor.
findings.fix_direction: Add pure-logic boot partial-frame test now; add NVS-corruption, ADC-rail, and Hall-EMI checks to the first-power hardware checklist.

findings.title: Wokwi CRSF-at-420000-baud over loopback is itself an unverified platform assumption the whole sim rests on
findings.evidence: SIMULATION.md 'First-run verify checklist' lists as UNCHECKED: 'CRSF decodes at 420000 baud over the loopback ... Fallback if not: sim-only baud override build flag', plus pin-label names, pot preset, and firmware.bin boot format. The baseline says esp32dev_sim BUILDS but there is no evidence it has been RUN successfully.
findings.why_matters: The control-fw sim is repeatedly cited as the primary Stage-2 confidence, but if Wokwi cannot clock the virtual UART at 420k or needs a merged bin, the sim silently proves nothing (the firmware would sit in permanent failsafe, which superficially still 'looks' plausible). The confidence attributed to the sim is provisional until someone confirms a `failsafe=0` line actually appears.
findings.expected: A recorded successful run (serial log showing the phase progression reaching DRIVING with failsafe=0) or a CI wokwi-cli run.
findings.suspected: The sim may never have produced a live link; its value is asserted but unconfirmed.
findings.how_to_verify: Run pio run -e esp32dev_sim then wokwi-cli and confirm a `[state] failsafe=0` line within ~3s, per the checklist item.
findings.fix_direction: Do one recorded Wokwi run and paste the serial log into SIMULATION.md; if 420k fails, add the sim-only baud-override flag mentioned.

findings.title: Ground-station renderer/main (hud.js, whep.js, CrsfSerialSource, main.js, mediamtx, telemetry.js) are entirely untested
findings.evidence: Tests import only shared/crsf.js, shared/crsfAssembler.js, shared/crsfTelemetry.js, shared/replaySource.js, shared/feelConstants.js. hud.js (246 lines), whep.js (89), main/CrsfSerialSource.js (96), main/main.js (106), main/mediamtx.js (54), and shared/telemetry.js are imported by no test. The 21 green tests cover pure decode/replay math only.
findings.why_matters: The HUD rendering (including the driveMode label 1=RACE/2=ERS that already disagrees with the firmware's 1=Gearbox/2=Gearbox+ERS per the baseline), the serialport wiring, WHEP video, and the actual telemetry-source selection are unproven. The green vitest count buys confidence only in the CRSF/replay decoders, not in what the operator actually sees.
findings.expected: At least a smoke test of CrsfSerialSource's assembler->frameToTelemetry pipeline against a fake serialport, and a unit test of hud.js DRIVE_MODES mapping.
findings.suspected: The HUD/serial/video layers rest on manual inspection; the driveMode label mismatch would not be caught here.
findings.how_to_verify: grep test/ imports — none reference main/ or renderer/ or shared/telemetry.js.
findings.fix_direction: Add a fake-serialport test for CrsfSerialSource and a DOM/unit test for hud.js label mapping; out of firmware scope but cheap.

open_questions: Has the control-fw Wokwi sim ever actually been run to a live link (failsafe=0), or only built? The entire Stage-2 confidence claim depends on this and the SIMULATION.md checklist boxes are all unchecked.

Is the Hall ISR read() snapshot required to be coherent? WheelSpeed's period-based rpm math assumes count and period come from the same edge pair; the two-independent-atomic-loads design permits tearing — need to confirm whether the pure logic tolerates a mismatched pair.
Does soundlight's esp32dev_sim building in CI give any false sense of a 'sim' when no wokwi files exist to run it? Was a diagram.json intended and dropped?

The two ci.yml firmware workflows are per-repo; is there any orchestration that would catch link2 protocol drift between the sender (control) and receiver (soundlight) copies, or the ground-station's third copy of the constants/labels?

Ground-station CI pins Node 20 but the baseline notes local Node is v26 and the app targets Electron ^31 / serialport ^12 — does the test environment match the runtime environment closely enough for the serial layer (which is untested anyway)?

hw_validation_items.item: Run the control-fw Wokwi sim once end-to-end and confirm the serial log reaches the DRIVING phase with failsafe=0 (validates the 420000-baud loopback assumption the whole Stage-2 story rests on).
On the bench, scope the Hall (GPIO35) line while the ESC runs at load; confirm rpm does not spike from EMI riding the slow edge, and that the 2ms ISR lockout absorbs bounce (untested in any sim).

Verify the ADC battery divider across extremes: disconnected/open divider, and full 8.4V; confirm analogReadMilliVolts + 4-read averaging gives sane batteryMv and no spurious low-voltage latch (ADC rails untested).
Corrupt the NVS settings partition (write garbage) and power-cycle; confirm the guard chain falls back to kDefaults rather than applying junk (Esp32NvsStore real-flash path untested).

On board #2, verify I2S audio at 22050 Hz through the legacy IDF driver on the pinned espressif32 ~7.0.1, and that WS2812 strip.show() does not glitch while audio DMA runs (dual-core race + DMA/RMT interaction has NO sim coverage).

Verify the real link2 UART RX on board #2 (control TX GPIO25 -> board#2 RX GPIO16, 115200 8N1, common ground) decodes frames — the soundlight sim bypasses the UART, so on-chip framing/overrun is completely unproven.
Confirm board #2 recovers gracefully if the first bytes it ever receives are mid-frame (power-on ordering with board #1 already transmitting) — boots NeverConnected then syncs on the next start byte.

End-to-end telemetry return path (battery/GPS/FLIGHTMODE frames up the ELRS backchannel to the ground station HUD) — bench-gated behind the FT232 COM port; verify the HUD driveMode label actually matches the firmware's mode numbering under live data.
